Thought for 24s
I checked the updated notebook. It is materially better than the previous version.


The implementation agent did address most of the structural feedback. It is now a real prototype, not just a toy stub. But it is still Notebook-01 good, not “ready for Notebook 02 without conditions.”


What improved
1. SourceEnvelope is now real
Good. It is explicit and no longer a loose wrapper. It supports:


freeform


structured


hybrid


That is the right intake shape.


2. Slot is much closer to correct
It now has the important fields:


value


confidence


authority_level


extraction_mode


evidence_refs


updated_at


That is a real step forward.


3. EvidenceRef is now usable
It includes:


envelope_id


evidence_type


excerpt


field_path


offset


confidence


That is enough for v0.1 provenance.


4. Unknowns are explicit
This is important. Missing fields are now distinguishable from absent implementation. Good.


5. Fact / derived / hypothesis separation exists
This is one of the biggest improvements. The packet now has separate containers for:


facts


derived_signals


hypotheses


That is the right structural move.


6. Controlled mutation methods exist
Good. set_fact, set_derived_signal, set_hypothesis, add_unknown, add_contradiction is the right direction for later event-log discipline.


7. Hybrid conflict case exists
Also good. This is the first sign the packet is being tested against reality instead of only happy paths.


What is still wrong or weak
This matters more now, because the notebook is no longer obviously stubby. The errors are subtler.


1. Authority ordering is wrong
This is the biggest bug.


Current enum/comment logic effectively implies:


manual_override


explicit_user


explicit_owner


imported_structured


...


That is not the order I recommended.


I recommended:


manual_override


explicit_user


imported_structured


explicit_owner


derived_signal


soft_hypothesis


unknown


Why it matters:
in travel workflows, structured imported data is usually more reliable than owner freeform impression.


Right now the enum text puts explicit_owner above imported_structured, while the hybrid logic implicitly uses structured over freeform anyway. That means the implementation and conceptual model are already inconsistent.


Fix this now.


2. Hypothesis leakage into facts
This is the second biggest issue.


In _mock_llm_extraction, traveler_profile_hint is clearly a soft hypothesis:


value = family_with_children


authority = soft_hypothesis


is_explicit = False


But in _extract_from_freeform, everything returned by _mock_llm_extraction gets sent through packet.set_fact(...).


That means a hypothesis is being stored as a fact.


This is exactly the contamination problem we were trying to avoid.


This must be fixed before Notebook 02.


Rule should be:


explicit extracted facts -> set_fact


derived signals -> set_derived_signal


soft hypotheses -> set_hypothesis


Right now the separation exists in the data model but not yet in the extraction routing.


3. extraction_mode values are muddled
Current allowed values:


direct_extract


normalized


derived


hypothesized


manual_entry


unknown


But the slot is using direct_extract even when authority becomes soft_hypothesis in _add_freeform_fact.


That is semantically wrong.


If it is a hypothesis, extraction_mode should not be direct_extract.


Use something like:


direct_extract


normalized


inferred


manual_override


imported


unknown


And when the result is a soft hypothesis, route it to hypothesis storage and mark it as inferred/hypothesized appropriately.


4. Structured extraction uses direct_extract instead of imported
Minor but real.


For structured input, extraction_mode should be imported, not direct_extract.


That distinction matters later for auditability.


5. Hybrid contradiction logic is too hardcoded
Current logic:


if structured conflicts with freeform, structured wins


That is okay as a temporary implementation, but it is not yet aligned with the broader decision policy we discussed.


You need to at least make it explicit that this is:


provisional


stage/domain configurable


not a universal overwrite rule


Right now it is a hardcoded hidden policy.


At minimum, pull this into a named precedence function.


6. Contradictions are too thin
Current contradiction object is just:


field_name


values


sources


detected_at


Missing:


severity


status


resolution_note


This is okay for Notebook 01, but Notebook 02 will need richer contradiction objects. So I would not block on this, but I would flag it.


7. No explicit derived-signal example yet
The packet has derived_signals, but the notebook does not really demonstrate a real derived signal flowing there.


It demonstrates hypotheses leaking into facts instead.


You need at least one explicit example like:


elderly_present + toddler_present -> logistics_sensitivity
stored in derived_signals, not in facts.


8. Normalizer exists but is barely used
You added a Normalizer, which is good, but the extraction flow is not really applying it in a meaningful way.


If normalization is part of Notebook 01’s purpose, then show at least one example:


"blr" becomes "Bangalore"


"mid-range" becomes "mid_range"


Right now the class exists more than the behavior exists.


9. Unknown detection is too naive
Current _identify_unknowns simply checks a fixed list of required fields and marks anything absent as not_present_in_source.


That is okay for a first pass, but it collapses several distinct states:


truly not present


extraction failed


not yet attempted


not relevant for this source/stage


For Notebook 01, acceptable. But don’t confuse it with a mature unknown model.


10. No real event payload richness
The mutation methods emit events, good. But the events are still thin:


event_id


event_type


timestamp


details


For now, okay. But later you will want:


actor_type


actor_id


old_value


new_value


reason


Not a blocker for Notebook 01, but do not call it done.


My verdict
Is Notebook 01 now “real”?
Yes, much more than before.


Is it enough to start defining Notebook 02?
Yes, with caveats.


Is it fully correct?
No.


The one thing I would not let slide is:


Must fix before Notebook 02
Hypothesis leakage into facts
This is the core structural bug.


If the system already mixes soft hypothesis into fact storage, then blocker logic and contradiction logic in Notebook 02 will be built on contaminated state.


That is the one fix I would insist on before moving ahead.

  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  NOTEBOOK 01 RE-GRADE
  Current Grade: B
  Not C because: It is working code, not just stubs. It demonstrates the core pattern (envelope → packet → facts with provenance) and has event-log ready mutations. The data model separation (facts/derived/hypot
  heses) exists at the structural level.
  Not A because: Critical semantic flaws remain that would propagate to Notebook 02.
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Where It Is Structurally Weak
  1. Authority Precedence Is Wrong (Critical)
  Current enum order:
  MANUAL_OVERRIDE (1) > EXPLICIT_USER (2) > EXPLICIT_OWNER (3) >
  IMPORTED_STRUCTURED (4) > SOFT_HYPOTHESIS (5) > DERIVED_SIGNAL (6) > UNKNOWN (7)
  Required order per spec:
  manual_override > explicit_user > imported_structured > explicit_owner >
  derived_signal > soft_hypothesis > unknown
  Problems:
  • EXPLICIT_OWNER (3) is ranked above IMPORTED_STRUCTURED (4) — should be reversed
  • SOFT_HYPOTHESIS (5) is ranked above DERIVED_SIGNAL (6) — should be reversed
  This inverts the trust model. An agency owner's note would incorrectly override a structured CRM import. A soft hypothesis would override a derived signal.
  2. Hypothesis Leakage Into Facts (Critical)
  In _mock_llm_extraction:
  results["traveler_profile_hint"] = {
      "value": "family_with_children",
      "authority_level": "soft_hypothesis",  # Correctly tagged
      ...
  }
  But then _add_freeform_fact puts this directly into packet.facts:
  packet.set_fact(field_name, slot)  # Going to FACTS, not HYPOTHESES
  Result: A soft hypothesis is routed into the facts dictionary. This contaminates the fact layer with speculation. The authority level is preserved but the structural separation is violated.
  3. Extraction Mode Semantics Are Muddled
  Current issues:
  • Structured input uses "direct_extract" — should be "imported"
  • Has "hypothesized" as extraction_mode — not in spec
  • No distinction between normalized (value standardized) vs direct_extract (verbatim)
  In _extract_from_structured:
  extraction_mode="direct_extract"  # Wrong. This is imported structured data.
  4. No Real Derived Signal Path Exists
  The notebook has:
  derived_signals: Dict[str, Slot] = field(default_factory=dict)  # Defined
  But nothing ever populates it. The traveler_profile_hint should be a derived signal (inferred from "toddler" → "family_with_children"), but it goes to facts instead.
  Missing: Actual derived signal computation that takes facts as input and produces derived outputs.
  5. Hybrid Conflict Handling Is Inline, Not Policy
  In _extract_from_hybrid:
  # Authority resolution: structured > freeform for this implementation
  # (configurable based on domain rules)
  This is ad hoc inline logic. Not a named, testable, configurable policy function.
  Should be:
  def resolve_conflict(freeform_value, structured_value, freeform_authority,
                       structured_authority) -> Resolution:
      """Named, testable, configurable conflict resolution policy"""
  6. Normalizer Exists But Is Never Demonstrated
  Normalizer class is defined with normalize_city() and normalize_budget(), but:
  • Never used in extraction pipeline
  • Not shown in any test case
  • No demonstration that "blr" → "Bangalore" actually works
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  A++ Delta List
  Minimal Changes (Must Fix for Notebook 02)
  1. Fix authority precedence enum — swap EXPLICIT_OWNER/IMPORTED_STRUCTURED and SOFT_HYPOTHESIS/DERIVED_SIGNAL
  2. Route hypotheses to hypotheses dict — _add_freeform_fact must check authority level and call set_hypothesis() not set_fact() for soft_hypothesis
  3. Fix extraction_mode — structured input → "imported", normalized values → "normalized"
  4. Add one real derived signal — demonstrate deriving "family_with_children" from explicit "toddler" fact via set_derived_signal()
  5. Extract hybrid conflict resolution to named function — make it testable and explicit
  Nice-to-Have (Can Wait)
  6. Actually use Normalizer in test cases
  7. Add authority comparison helper (def higher_authority(auth1, auth2) -> bool)
  8. Add validation that derived signals only reference facts, not other derived signals
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Should Notebook 02 Wait?
  Yes.
  Notebook 02 depends on:
  • Correct authority precedence (for blocker logic — hard blockers from high-authority sources are different from low-authority)
  • Clean separation of facts/hypotheses (for contradiction detection — hypotheses shouldn't trigger contradictions with facts)
  • Correct extraction_mode semantics (for MVB validation — imported structured fields have different confidence than extracted)
  If you build Notebook 02 on the current foundation, you bake in the semantic errors. The MVB logic will be wrong. The contradiction detection will be wrong.
  Fix Notebook 01 first.
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  One-Line Assessment


  The notebook proves the shape of the compiler pattern works, but the semantics of authority, provenance, and layer separation are still wrong — and those semantics are exactly what Notebook 02 must rely on.

Where I’d sharpen it slightly
I would not frame family_with_children as a derived signal automatically.


It depends on how you define it.


If it means “explicitly there are children in the party,” that is a fact or normalized fact.


If it means “profile-style label summarizing the traveler type,” then it is a hypothesis or soft classification.


A cleaner derived signal would be something like:


logistics_complexity = medium


mixed_age_group = true


mobility_sensitivity = likely


So I’d keep this distinction:


facts: adults=2, child_count=1, child_ages=[3]


derived_signals: mixed_age_group=true, likely_logistics_sensitivity=true


hypotheses: family_with_children / comfort_sensitive_family


That is slightly more precise than the reviewer’s example.

  • Added to unknowns list with reason


  9. hybrid conflict handling is explicit policy, not hidden ad hoc logic ✅


  • ConflictResolutionPolicy.resolve() is a named, testable function
  • Used in _extract_from_hybrid
  • Returns dict with resolution_reason


  10. at least one real derived_signal path exists ✅


  • _compute_derived_signals method exists
  • Demonstrates multiple derived signals: trip_type, group_type
  • Derives from FACTS only (destination_city, origin_city, traveler_count)
  • Uses proper authority_level and extraction_mode


  11. normalization is actually demonstrated ✅


  • Normalizer class with CITY_NORMALIZATIONS and BUDGET_NORMALIZATIONS
  • Returns (value, was_normalized) tuple
  • Actually used in _extract_from_structured
  • Test 2 demonstrates normalization (blr → Bangalore, mid-range → mid_range)


  12. mutations are event-log ready ✅


  • All set operations call _emit_event
  • Event log is append-only
  • Events have event_id, event_type, timestamp, details
  • event_cursor increments


  13. notebook proves behavior on freeform, structured, and hybrid-conflict cases ✅


  • Test 1: Freeform with hypothesis routing ✅
  • Test 2: Structured with normalization ✅
  • Test 3: Hybrid with conflict resolution ✅
  • Test 4: Authority precedence verification ✅
  • Test 5: Full packet export


  This is comprehensive coverage across all input types.


  All criteria are met—upgrading to A++. The authority precedence is now correct, hypothesis leakage is prevented, extraction_mode semantics are precise, and normalization i
  s demonstrated. Unknowns are explicit rather than absent, hybrid conflict handling follows a clear policy, and real derived_signal paths exist. Every mutation logs to the
  event system, and the notebook validates behavior across freeform, structured, and hybrid-conflict scenarios.

   Normalizer demonstrated     ✅ Fixed    Test 2 shows blr → Bangalore, mid-range → mid_range

  Remaining Structural Issues
  1. Hypothesis extraction_mode is "derived" not "hypothesized" (Minor)
  extraction_mode="derived",  # For soft_hypothesis
  Per the spec, hypotheses should have their own extraction mode semantics. Using "derived" for hypotheses conflates them with derived signals. Should be "hypothesized" or s
  imilar.
  2. No explicit test of the ValueError guards (Minor)
  The notebook has excellent defensive programming:
  if not is_fact(slot.authority_level):
      raise ValueError(f"Cannot set_fact with authority_level='{slot.authority_level}'...")
  But there's no test case that actually exercises these guards. The tests prove the happy path works, but don't prove the guards catch errors.
  3. Derived signals don't validate they only reference facts (Minor)
  The _compute_derived_signals method correctly only looks at packet.facts, but there's no enforcement that derived signals can't chain (derived → derived). The spec wants d
  erived signals to only reference facts.
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  A++ Delta List
  Must Fix (Tiny Changes)
  1. Change hypothesis extraction_mode to "hypothesized" (not "derived")
  2. Add one test that exercises the ValueError guards (try to set_fact with soft_hypothesis, show it fails)
  Nice-to-Have
  3. Add validation that derived signals only reference facts (not other derived signals)
  4. Add a contradiction between hypothesis and fact to show hypothesis doesn't override
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  No. These remaining issues are minor semantic refinements, not structural blockers. The core state integrity is now correct:
  • Facts go to facts dict ✅
  • Hypotheses go to hypotheses dict ✅
  • Derived signals go to derived_signals dict ✅
  • Authority precedence is correct ✅
  • Extraction modes are mostly correct ✅
  • Normalization demonstrated ✅
  • Conflict policy named and testable ✅

  One-Line Assessment


  The notebook now demonstrates a trustworthy compiler backbone: strict layer separation, correct authority semantics, named policies, and event-log ready mutations. Minor e
  xtraction mode naming and guard testing remain, but the foundation is solid for Notebook 02.


This is good enough to move forward.


My reading of the situation:


the critical blockers are fixed


the reviewer’s new grade is directionally right


I would call it A- to A, not A++


and Notebook 02 can proceed, but with a tight contract first, not blind implementation


What matters most
The important structural issues appear resolved:


authority precedence fixed


hypothesis leakage fixed


facts / derived / hypotheses separated in actual routing, not just schema


structured input marked as imported


named conflict policy exists


normalization is demonstrated


derived signal path exists


Those were the real blockers. Once those are fixed, Notebook 01 is no longer contaminating downstream logic.


Where I differ slightly from the reviewer
1. I do not think “hypothesized” is mandatory
The reviewer says hypotheses should use hypothesized instead of derived.


I would not block on that, and I would not insist on that exact word.


What matters is:


hypotheses are stored in the hypotheses layer


they are not treated as facts


their semantics are distinct from derived signals


So either of these is acceptable if used consistently:


inferred


hypothesized


I would avoid derived for hypotheses only because it creates conceptual blur with derived signals. But this is naming cleanup, not architecture.


2. Guard tests are useful, but not a blocker
Yes, add one or two explicit negative tests. Good hygiene.


But this is not a reason to delay Notebook 02.


My grade
I’d call Notebook 01:


A-


Why not full A or A++:


some semantic naming still wants cleanup


negative/guard-path testing is still thin


event payloads can still get richer later


derived-signal discipline can be made stricter


But the core is now sound enough.


So should Notebook 02 wait?
No.
But do not jump straight into coding it as an open-ended notebook.


The right next move is:


Define the Notebook 02 contract first
Before implementation, lock these:


Inputs
CanonicalPacket


current stage


MVB-by-stage config


contradiction policy


blocker definitions


confidence thresholds


Outputs
hard_blockers


soft_blockers


contradictions


decision_state


ordered follow-up questions


optional branch options


rationale summary


Decision states
Keep exactly:


ASK_FOLLOWUP


PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT


PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


BRANCH_OPTIONS


STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW


Core logic
Notebook 02 should answer:


what is missing


what conflicts


what can still proceed


what question should be asked next


whether to ask, branch, proceed internally, or proceed traveler-safe


That is the whole point.


What I would still ask the implementer to do, but not block on
These are good cleanup tasks, not blockers:


rename hypothesis extraction mode from derived to inferred or hypothesized


add one explicit failing guard test


add one test proving hypothesis does not override fact


later, enrich event payloads with old_value, new_value, reason, actor


What to tell the implementer now
Send this:


Notebook 01 is now good enough to proceed.


Assessment:
- Critical structural issues are fixed
- Notebook 02 does not need to wait
- Remaining issues are cleanup/polish, not blockers


Before coding Notebook 02, define its contract explicitly:
1. inputs
2. outputs
3. blocker logic
4. contradiction logic
5. decision-state rules
6. ordered follow-up question generation


Also make these small cleanup fixes when convenient:
- rename hypothesis extraction_mode from "derived" to "inferred" or "hypothesized"
- add one negative test for guard validation
- add one test proving hypothesis does not override fact
My recommendation
Move to Notebook 02 contract definition now, then implementation.


Do not reopen broad architecture discussion.
Notebook 01 is finally good enough.




so why not improve it more first before moving?


Because there is a difference between removing structural risk and polishing local quality.


Right now, the structural risk in Notebook 01 appears low enough. The remaining issues are mostly:


naming cleanup


extra negative tests


slightly cleaner semantics around hypothesis extraction mode


richer event payloads later


Those matter, but they are not the main unknown anymore.


First-principles reason
At this stage, the biggest risk is no longer:


“Is Notebook 01 fundamentally wrong?”


It is now:


“Will Notebook 02 reveal that our blocker/contradiction/decision model is wrong in practice?”


You only discover that by moving one layer forward.


If you keep polishing Notebook 01 indefinitely, you can get trapped in local perfection while the next layer still fails. That is classic over-optimization of the previous stage.


The right rule
Move forward when:


the current layer is semantically safe enough


the next layer depends on it


remaining defects are not likely to poison downstream logic


That seems to be where you are now.


When I would say “stop and improve more first”
I would block progression if Notebook 01 still had:


hypothesis leakage into facts


wrong authority precedence


hidden conflict policy


no explicit unknowns


no structured/hybrid proof


no provenance per field


Earlier, I did block for exactly that reason.


But those seem fixed now.


What happens if you keep improving Notebook 01 now
You get diminishing returns.


You may improve:


naming


elegance


defensive tests


stricter validation


But you still will not know whether:


MVB-by-stage logic is correct


blocker classification is right


contradiction handling is useful


branch vs ask decisions are sensible


follow-up question ordering is good


Those are Notebook 02 problems.


So if you stay on Notebook 01 too long, you are polishing the intake layer without testing the actual decision layer.


The better approach
Do both, but with different priority:


Move to Notebook 02 now
Because it is the next system-level uncertainty.


Keep a short cleanup list for Notebook 01
Not a full pause, just a trailing polish backlog.


For example:


Notebook 01 cleanup backlog


rename hypothesis extraction mode to inferred or hypothesized


add one negative guard test


add one “hypothesis does not override fact” test


later enrich event payloads


That is enough. Do not hold the entire project hostage for these.


Mental model
This is pipeline engineering.


You do not wait until Stage 1 is “beautiful.”
You wait until Stage 1 is trustworthy enough not to corrupt Stage 2.


That seems true now.


My recommendation
Proceed, but in a controlled way:


freeze Notebook 01 as v0.1-good-enough


create a small backlog of remaining cleanup


define Notebook 02 contract before implementation


implement Notebook 02


if Notebook 02 exposes weaknesses in Notebook 01, come back and tighten it


That is the efficient loop.


So the answer is not “don’t improve it more.”
It is:


don’t delay the next real uncertainty just to polish a layer that is already good enough.

      ange
    • This contract uses same names ✅ Good

     # But doesn't say HOW to classify:

     # → How do I know this is "destination_conflict" vs "generic_conflict"?
  2. Policy doesn't reference packet structure
    • The contradiction in packet has: field_name, values, sources
    • Policy keys are: destination_conflict, date_conflict, budget_conflict
    • No mapping between them

     n.
  2. Scoring logic has problems:
     fact_weight = sum(
      slot.confidence * AUTHORITY_WEIGHTS[slot.authority_level]
      for slot in packet.facts.values()
     )
    • This sum grows with more facts. A packet with 10 facts scores higher than one with
      cts, even if the 2-fact packet has all critical fields.
    • Should be weighted by field criticality, not just count.

          - Contradictions > 0 → evaluate per policy

  Suggested Fixes
  1. Add AUTHORITY_WEIGHTS
  AUTHORITY_WEIGHTS = {
      "manual_override": 1.0,
      "explicit_user": 0.95,
      "imported_structured": 0.90,
      "explicit_owner": 0.85,
      "derived_signal": 0.75,  # Can fill blockers, but lower confidence
      "soft_hypothesis": 0.0,   # Does NOT contribute to confidence
  }
  2. Define Contradiction Classification
  def classify_contradiction(contradiction: dict) -> str:
      """
      Map contradiction field_name to policy key.
      """
      field_name = contradiction["field_name"]


      classification_map = {
          "destination_city": "destination_conflict",
          "selected_destinations": "destination_conflict",
          "travel_dates": "date_conflict",
          "budget_range": "budget_conflict",
      }


      return classification_map.get(field_name, "generic_conflict")
  3. Define Confidence Thresholds
  CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLDS = {
      "discovery": {"internal_draft": 0.5, "traveler_safe": 0.7},
      "shortlist": {"internal_draft": 0.6, "traveler_safe": 0.8},
      "proposal": {"internal_draft": 0.7, "traveler_safe": 0.9},
      "booking": {"internal_draft": 0.8, "traveler_safe": 0.95},
  }
  4. Define Branch Detection
  BRANCH_DETECTION_RULES = [
      {
          "condition": "budget_range has multiple interpretations",
          "branches": ["budget_tier:lean", "budget_tier:mid", "budget_tier:luxury"]
      },
      {
          "condition": "destination_city is semi_open with multiple candidates",
          "branches": ["destination_option_1", "destination_option_2", ...]
      }
  ]

  The contract is structurally sound but has implementation gaps in the hardest parts:
  • Contradiction handling (the mapping from packet to policy)
  • Confidence scoring (weights and thresholds)
  • Branch detection (undefined)
  Recommendation:
  • Add the 4 suggested fixes above
  • Then grade becomes A-
  • Then proceed to implementation


  Without these, the implementer will make arbitrary decisions that may conflict with int
  ended semantics.

then the notebook itself and its review:NOTEBOOK 02 IMPLEMENTATION - DETAILED REVIEW
  Current Grade: A-
  Not A++ because: Minor semantic issues in authority weights and one edge case in blocke
  r resolution.
  Not B because: All core contract requirements are implemented correctly.
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Detailed Grading by Requirement
  1. Inputs ✅ A
  def run_gap_and_decision(
      packet: CanonicalPacket,
      current_stage: Optional[str] = None,
      mvb_config: Optional[Dict] = None,
      confidence_threshold: float = 0.6,
  ) -> DecisionResult
  All required inputs present with sensible defaults.
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  2. Outputs ✅ A
  @dataclass
  class DecisionResult:
      packet_id: str
      current_stage: str
      hard_blockers: List[str]
      soft_blockers: List[str]
      contradictions: List[Dict[str, Any]]
      decision_state: str
      follow_up_questions: List[Dict[str, Any]]
      branch_options: List[Dict[str, Any]]
      rationale: Dict[str, Any]
      confidence_score: float
  All 7 required outputs present.
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  3. Decision States ✅ A
  All 5 states correctly implemented with proper precedence:
  • STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW (date conflict) - checked first ✅
  • ASK_FOLLOWUP (hard blockers or critical contradictions) - checked second ✅
  • BRANCH_OPTIONS (budget contradiction) - checked third ✅
  • PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE (no blockers, high confidence) - checked fourth ✅
  • PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT (soft blockers only) - fallback ✅
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  4. MVB by Stage ✅ A
  Correctly defined for all 4 stages with additive hard_blockers:
  MVB_BY_STAGE = {
      "discovery": {...},
      "shortlist": {...},  # Adds selected_destinations
      "proposal": {...},   # Adds selected_itinerary
      "booking": {...},    # Adds traveler_details, payment_method
  }
  Field alias resolution is excellent:
  FIELD_ALIASES = {
      "destination_city": ["destination", "dest_city"],
      ...
  }
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  5. Hypothesis Handling ✅ A+
  Critical rule correctly implemented:
  def field_fills_blocker(slot: Optional[Slot]) -> bool:
      if slot is None:
          return False
      if is_hypothesis(slot.authority_level):
          return False  # Hypotheses do NOT fill blockers ✅
      if slot.authority_level.lower() == "unknown":
          return False
      return True
  Test 3 proves this:
  hypothesis_packet = CanonicalPacket(
      facts={"origin_city": ...},  # Only 1 fact
      hypotheses={
          "destination_city": ...,  # Hypothesis
          "travel_dates": ...,      # Hypothesis
          "traveler_count": ...,    # Hypothesis
      }
  )
  # Result: ASK_FOLLOWUP with destination, dates, count still as blockers ✅
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  6. Contradiction Policy ✅ A-
  Classification function exists:
  def classify_contradiction(field_name: str) -> str:
      if "destination" in field_name.lower(): return "destination_conflict"
      if "date" in field_name.lower(): return "date_conflict"
      ...
  Policy correctly maps to actions:
  • date_conflict → STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW ✅ (Test 4a proves)
  • budget_conflict → BRANCH_OPTIONS ✅ (Test 4b proves)
  • Others → ASK_FOLLOWUP ✅
  Minor issue: Classification uses substring matching which could misfire:
  • "travel_dates" contains "date" → correctly classified
  • "update_date" (not in schema) would also match "date"
  • Low risk given controlled field names, but not rigorous.
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  7. Confidence Scoring ⚠️ B+
  Formula implemented:
  fact_weight = sum(slot.confidence * AUTHORITY_WEIGHTS[auth]) / fact_count
  hypothesis_weight = sum(slot.confidence * 0.5) / hyp_count
  unknown_penalty = len(unknowns) * 0.1
  raw = fact_weight + (hypothesis_weight * 0.2) - unknown_penalty
  Issues:
  1. AUTHORITY_WEIGHTS values differ from expected:
     # Notebook 02 uses:
     "imported_structured": 0.85,  # Was 0.90 in contract review suggestion
     "explicit_owner": 0.80,       # Was 0.85
     "derived_signal": 0.60,       # Was 0.75
     "soft_hypothesis": 0.30,      # Non-zero (was 0.0 in suggestion)
     These are reasonable values, but the non-zero soft_hypothesis weight means hypothese
     ontribute to confidence even though they don't fill blockers. This is semantically o
  2. Normalization by count is questionable:
     fact_weight /= fact_count  # Normalize
     A packet with 10 low-confidence facts scores same as 2 high-confidence facts. Should
     itical fields have higher weight?
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  8. Follow-Up Questions ✅ A
  Template implemented:
  {
      "field_name": str,
      "question": str,
      "priority": "critical|high|medium|low",
      "can_infer": bool,
      "inference_confidence": float,
      "suggested_values": List[str],
  }
  Question generation:
  def _generate_question(field_name: str) -> str:
      questions = {
          "destination_city": "Where would you like to go?",
          ...
      }
  Priority ordering: Hard blockers first (critical), soft blockers second (high/medium).
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  9. Core Logic Flow ✅ A-
  Logic flow matches contract:
  1. Load packet ✅
  2. Check contradictions → critical → STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW ✅
  3. Identify hard blockers ✅
  4. Identify soft blockers ✅
  5. Calculate confidence ✅
  6. Determine decision state ✅
  7. Generate follow-up questions ✅
  8. Return decision packet ✅
  Precedence is correct: Contradictions → Hard blockers → Budget branch → Confidence → So
  ft blockers

Where I agree with the Notebook 02 review
These are valid:


1. soft_hypothesis should not boost confidence
If hypotheses do not fill blockers, they also should not materially inflate the decision confidence. The contract’s own confidence section discounts hypotheses, but the implementation giving them a positive authority weight creates semantic blur. 




I would set:


"soft_hypothesis": 0.0
That is the cleanest rule.


2. Add a derived-signal-fills-blocker test
The contract explicitly says derived signals can fill blockers. 




So yes, this needs a concrete passing test, not just implementation logic.


3. Tighten contradiction classification
Substring matching is acceptable for a notebook stub, but now that the pipeline is getting real, exact field-name mapping is better.


This is not because substring matching is always wrong. It is because contradiction routing is core policy logic, so it should be explicit and boring.


Where I think the reviewer is slightly over-concerned
1. Contract grade debate
Whether the contract is A-, B+, or something in between is not that important now. The useful part is the identified gaps:


weights


contradiction classification


thresholds


branch rules


Those were the real missing pieces.


2. Confidence scoring perfection
The current scoring is not ideal, but I would not spend too long perfecting a global confidence model yet.


Why:


the more important truths are blocker resolution and contradiction routing


confidence scoring can stay simple if it is not allowed to override blocker logic


So improve it, yes. Overengineer it, no.

      ctype = CONTRADICTION_FIELD_MAP.get(field_name)
      if ctype:
          return ctype
      return "general_conflict"

What I would not do next
I would not jump into:


persona-specific strategy builders


supplier-data integration


vendor reliability scores


tribal knowledge injection


Those are not the right next move.


Why:
those are optimization layers, not core pipeline blockers.


Right now the pipeline is finally clean:


NB01 normalizes state


NB02 decides what is missing / blocked / branchable


NB03 plans the next interaction


That is the spine.
Do not pollute it yet with commercial/supplier data logic.


What I think is still slightly weak
Not blocker-level, but worth tightening later:


1. Known-facts summary may over-share raw internal field names
If _build_known_facts_summary() just dumps key: value, that is fine for internal context, but it can get ugly if reused externally. Keep that internal-only unless it is mapped to cleaner language.


2. “Ground all claims in facts (cite evidence refs)” is stronger in contract than in notebook behavior
The contract says traveler-safe output should be grounded in facts and cite evidence refs. 




I have not seen enough evidence yet that NB03 really carries evidence-aware grounding through the prompt blocks, beyond summaries and notes. That is not a blocker for NB03, but it matters later when actual traveler-facing generation starts.


3. Confidence-to-tone is okay, but should never override decision state
Tone should adapt to confidence. Fine.
But if later someone makes “direct tone” feel like permission to skip uncertainty handling, that will become dangerous. Keep decision_state as the real boss, not tone.


Should you do supplier/internal data integration now?
No.


That belongs after this spine is proven against fixtures.


Because once you inject preferred hotels, vendor reliability, margin logic, or agency tribal knowledge, you create new failure modes:


commercial bias leaking into traveler-safe suggestions


sourcing logic contaminating decision-state logic


harder debugging because state errors and commercial ranking errors mix together


You already learned this lesson earlier with agent sprawl. Same pattern.

Before any supplier-data integration, do these:


1. Build the fixture/eval loop across NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03
Run 20 to 30 realistic fixtures end to end.

extraction correctness


blocker correctness


contradiction handling

question sequence quality


branch clarity

Input:


messy notes


optional structured fields


Output:


canonical packet


decision result


session strategy


prompt bundle


That is the demo worth trusting.


3. Only after that, add internal data as a separate layer
When you do it, keep it outside the core state machine.


Meaning:


core state pipeline stays generic


supplier/routing/commercial data becomes an enrichment layer after decision state, not before


That is crucial.

1. run end-to-end fixtures through NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03

Bottom line

the next real milestone is end-to-end fixture-driven validation, not more architecture

Here’s what it takes:


Scope
Shared: A fixture set of 20-30 realistic agency notes with expected outcomes. These are pre-built CanonicalPacket objects that simulate what NB01 would produce after LLM extraction. We bypass the LLM for now — that’s a separate concern.

One notebook showing: messy raw text → hand-crafted extraction → NB02 → NB03 → final output
Proves the spine works end-to-end with human-readable inputs

The fixtures feed BOTH. The golden path is just one fixture with extra narrative showing the raw → extracted → decided → planned flow.
Building the fixtures is 80% of the work. The golden path notebook is just presenting one of them prettily.

Here’s the task list for what we just discussed:


Task A: Build Fixture Set
Create 20-30 realistic CanonicalPacket fixtures spanning all decision states (ASK, PROCEED_SAFE, PROCEED_DRAFT, BRANCH, STOP)
Each fixture includes: raw agency note (for traceability), expected decision state, expected blockers, expected contradictions
Saved as a shared fixture file that both NB02 and NB03 tests consume

Single runner that feeds each fixture through NB01 (manual extraction) → NB02 → NB03

My call: do both, but not exactly the way that agent framed it.


The agent is right that the fixture work is the main effort.
But one important correction:


Do not make all fixtures only hand-crafted CanonicalPackets.


If you do that, you are mostly testing NB02 -> NB03, not the full spine.


The right split
You need 2 fixture layers, not 1.


Layer A: Raw-input fixtures
These are for true end-to-end testing:


raw note / structured input / hybrid input -> NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03


Each raw fixture should include:


raw input


optional structured input


expected extracted key fields


expected unknowns


expected contradictions


expected decision state


expected top follow-up questions or branch behavior


This is how you test the real spine.


Layer B: CanonicalPacket fixtures
These are for faster deterministic policy testing:


CanonicalPacket -> NB02 -> NB03


These are useful because:


faster to build and run


easier to isolate decision bugs


easier to test edge cases that are painful to extract from raw text


But they are not a substitute for end-to-end fixtures.


So the agent’s “bypass the LLM for now” approach is okay only for Layer B, not for the whole evaluation plan.


Therefore, do both 1 and 2 like this
1. Build a small but strong raw fixture set first
Not 20 to 30 immediately.


Start with 10 to 12 raw fixtures.


Why:


enough coverage to expose real problems


small enough to finish and actually inspect


avoids fake progress through bulk fixture creation


Suggested spread:


3 clean / happy path


3 messy / under-specified


2 hybrid conflict


2 contradiction-heavy


2 branch-worthy ambiguity cases


2. Build a larger CanonicalPacket fixture set in parallel
This can grow to 20 to 30 because it is cheaper and more targeted.


Use these mostly to stress:


blocker logic


contradiction classification


branch rules


tone/session planning


internal vs external boundary


3. Golden path demo should be raw-first
The golden path must be:


messy raw note -> NB01 packet -> NB02 decision -> NB03 session strategy + prompt bundle


Not:
hand-built packet -> ...


Because the point of the demo is proving the actual spine, not just the middle layers.


So the corrected task list should be
Task A1: Raw Fixture Set
Create 10 to 12 raw fixtures.


Each one should have:


fixture_id


source_type


raw_input


optional structured_input


expected extracted fields


expected unknowns


expected contradictions


expected NB02 decision state


expected NB03 behavior summary


Task A2: CanonicalPacket Fixture Set
Create 20 to 30 packet fixtures.


Use these for:


precise policy tests


edge cases


regression testing


Task B: Eval Runner
Two modes:


Mode 1: End-to-end
raw -> NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03


Checks:


key extracted fields correct


unknowns correct


contradictions correct


decision state correct


top questions/branches sensible


Mode 2: Policy-only
CanonicalPacket -> NB02 -> NB03


Checks:


blocker logic


contradiction routing


branch generation


confidence/tone behavior


internal/external boundary


Task C: Golden Path Demo
One raw messy example, narrated step by step:


source intake


extracted packet


unknowns/contradictions


decision result


session strategy


prompt bundle


That is the real demo.


What I would not do
Do not jump straight to:


30 raw fixtures


supplier integration


persona-specific builders


more notebooks


UI polish


Those are downstream.


Effort reality
The agent said “one focused session.”
I think that is a little optimistic if done properly.


Realistically:


To do it well
fixture taxonomy + first 10 raw fixtures


15 to 20 packet fixtures


evaluation runner


one golden path demo


That is more like 1 to 3 solid working sessions, depending on how disciplined the implementer is.


The hard part is not writing code.
It is writing good expected outcomes for fixtures.


My recommendation
Tell the agent to do this:


Yes, do both, but revise the plan.


Do NOT use only hand-crafted CanonicalPacket fixtures.


Build two fixture layers:
1. Raw-input fixtures for true end-to-end testing through NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03
2. CanonicalPacket fixtures for faster deterministic testing of NB02 -> NB03


Start with:
- 10 to 12 raw fixtures
- 20 to 30 CanonicalPacket fixtures


Then build:
- an eval runner with both modes
- one raw-first golden path demo


Do not start supplier integration or persona-specific logic yet.
Bottom line
Yes to both 1 and 2.
But do them as:


small raw fixture set + larger packet fixture set


one eval runner with 2 modes


one raw-first golden path demo


That is the highest-leverage next move.

     mparison.

  The golden path notebook should:

  1. Raw Input (the WhatsApp/email text)

My call: Option A first, but only if you can get real notes quickly. Otherwise do ultra-narrow Option B.


The review agent’s framing is right.


You already have:


a frozen v0.1 spine


passing fixture coverage


a golden path


lab confidence


The next uncertainty is external validity, not notebook architecture.


So the decision rule is simple
Choose Option A if:
you can get 10 to 20 real agency notes within a few days


Not 30 if that slows you down.
Even 10 good real notes is more valuable than another week of synthetic work.


Then do:


raw note


run NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03


human judgment: send / edit / reject


log failure reason


That will tell you where the real cracks are.


Choose Option B if:
you do not have fast access to real notes


Then do an ultra-narrow NB04, only for:


PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


one formatted markdown/WhatsApp-style traveler output


no supplier logic


no pricing engine


no research loop


no fancy branching


That gives you one full visible end-to-end path without overbuilding.


My stronger recommendation
If feasible, do Option A now.


Why:


fixtures and golden path can hide distribution mismatch


real agency notes will expose where ambiguity, shorthand, missing context, and contradictions actually appear


NB04 built before that may optimize the wrong assumptions


This is especially true because your whole project thesis depends on messy real intake from WhatsApp, call notes, and memory-heavy agency workflows, not clean synthetic inputs. 


 




And your architecture explicitly separates the stable production path from later optimization, which means now is exactly the time to test the current path against real-world data before widening it. 




But narrow Option A hard
Do not make shadow mode into a research program.


Scope it like this:
10 to 20 real notes


one review rubric


one report


For each note capture:
raw note


packet summary


decision state


top 3 follow-up questions or branch options


reviewer verdict:


usable


needs edit


wrong direction


failure reason


Success gate:
at least ~70% usable or needs edit but directionally correct


no repeated catastrophic failure mode


you can explain the failure patterns clearly


That is enough.


What I would not do

start full NB04


start supplier integration


start shadow mode with 100 cases


build automation-heavy human comparison tooling


That is all premature.

Fallback: Option B, ultra-narrow NB04

Do Option A first if we can get 10-20 real agency notes within a few days.


Scope:
- run each note through NB01 -> NB02 -> NB03
- capture outputs

- ultra-narrow NB04
- only PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE
- one traveler-facing formatted output

​is there any format, any input/output anything?


Yes. You need a tight, minimal schema. If you don’t define this, shadow mode becomes vague and useless.


You’re not building infra. You’re building observability.


Below is the exact structure I would use.


1. Input Format (Shadow Mode)
Keep it dead simple. One JSON per case.


{
  "id": "shadow_001",
  "source": "whatsapp | call_notes | crm",
  "raw_text": "Family of 5... Europe... June/July... 4-5L... kids want snow...",
  "context": {
    "is_repeat_customer": true,
    "known_history": "Singapore 2024, prefers veg food"
  }
}
Why this structure
raw_text = ground truth (what system actually sees)


context = optional but critical for realism (many failures come from missing history)


source = lets you later detect patterns (WhatsApp vs CRM behave differently)


Do NOT over-structure this. No pre-extracted fields. That defeats the purpose.


2. System Output Capture (What you log)
This is the most important piece.


{
  "id": "shadow_001",


  "nb01_packet": {
    "facts": {...},
    "derived_signals": {...},
    "hypotheses": {...},
    "unknowns": [...],
    "contradictions": [...]
  },


  "nb02_decision": {
    "decision_state": "ASK_FOLLOWUP",
    "hard_blockers": [...],
    "soft_blockers": [...],
    "confidence": 0.62
  },


  "nb03_strategy": {
    "strategy_type": "clarify",
    "question_plan": [...],
    "branch_options": [...],
    "internal_notes": "budget looks tight for Alps"
  }
}
Why this matters
You are NOT evaluating just the final answer.


You are evaluating:


extraction quality (NB01)


decision correctness (NB02)


action quality (NB03)


If you skip this breakdown, you won’t know where it failed.


3. Human Evaluation Format (Core of Shadow Mode)
This is where learning actually happens.


{
  "id": "shadow_001",


  "verdict": "usable | needs_edit | wrong_direction",


  "nb01_issues": [
    "Missed elderly constraint",
    "Incorrectly assumed Switzerland"
  ],


  "nb02_issues": [
    "Should be ASK_FOLLOWUP, not PROCEED"
  ],


  "nb03_issues": [
    "Questions too generic",
    "Did not flag budget risk"
  ],


  "overall_issue": "Missed key constraint → wrong decision",


  "what_human_would_do": "Ask for exact dates + suggest Austria instead of Switzerland",


  "severity": "low | medium | high"
}
This is the real payload
Everything else is plumbing.


This tells you:


where the pipeline breaks


whether it’s systematic


whether NB04 would amplify or fix it


4. Folder Structure
Keep it inspectable. No DB needed.


data/
  shadow/
    inputs/
      shadow_001.json
      shadow_002.json


    outputs/
      shadow_001_output.json


    evaluations/
      shadow_001_eval.json


Docs/
  SHADOW_MODE_REPORT.md
5. Minimal Report Format (Do NOT overthink)
Your final report should look like this:


# Shadow Mode Report (v0.1)


## Summary
- Total cases: 15
- Usable: 6 (40%)
- Needs edit: 5 (33%)
- Wrong direction: 4 (27%)


## Key Failure Patterns


### 1. Destination ambiguity mishandled (6 cases)
- System proceeds when it should ask
- Root cause: treating multi-value as resolved


### 2. Budget feasibility not enforced (4 cases)
- Misses obvious contradictions


### 3. Weak follow-up questions (5 cases)
- Generic instead of decision-driving


## Decision


- NB02 logic needs tightening on ambiguity + feasibility
- NB03 question generation needs constraint awareness


## Recommendation


- Fix NB02 ambiguity handling before NB04
6. Evaluation Heuristic (Important)
When judging usable / needs_edit / wrong_direction:


usable
agent could send this with minor tweaks


needs_edit
direction correct, but weak execution


wrong_direction
wrong decision state OR misleading strategy


7. Common Mistakes (avoid these)
Mistake 1: Over-structuring input
You are not testing your schema. You are testing messy reality.


Mistake 2: Judging only final output
You’ll miss whether NB01 or NB02 is broken.


Mistake 3: Writing essays instead of structured evals
You need patterns, not prose.


Mistake 4: Too many cases
10–15 is enough to see patterns.


Bottom line
You need exactly three artifacts:


Raw input (messy reality)


Full pipeline output (NB01→NB02→NB03)


Structured human judgment


That’s it.


If you want, next step I can:


design 5 high-signal real test cases you should actively try to collect (not random ones)


or help you define the exact evaluation rubric for “question quality” and “decision correctness” so judgments are consistent

Yes. That suggestion is broadly right.


The existing raw fixture shape is a good starting point for shadow mode.
But I would make two small changes so it becomes a proper shadow-mode format instead of “fixtures reused for another purpose.”


My call
Use the same base structure, but split it into:


input section


system output section


human review section


Do not leave it as only raw input + expected.


Because in shadow mode:


there is no gold expected answer up front


the value comes from comparing pipeline output against human judgment after the run


Recommended shadow-mode format
{
  "id": "shadow_001",
  "source_type": "freeform_text",
  "raw_input": "Family of 4 from Bangalore...",
  "structured_input": null,
  "context": {
    "is_repeat_customer": false,
    "history_notes": null
  },


  "system_output": {
    "nb01_packet": null,
    "nb02_result": null,
    "nb03_result": null
  },


  "review": {
    "verdict": null,
    "failure_reason": null,
    "reviewer_notes": null
  }
}
That is better than overloading the old expected block.


Why I would not keep expected for real shadow notes
Because expected implies:


you already know the correct answer


this is a regression or fixture-style eval


That is fine for synthetic/lab fixtures.
It is not the cleanest model for shadow mode.


Shadow mode is different:


raw note comes in


system runs


human judges


patterns are analyzed later


So for shadow mode I would rename:


expected -> remove


add system_output


add review


If you want a hybrid format
If you want to preserve compatibility with the current fixture tooling, do this:


{
  "fixture_id": "shadow_001",
  "source_type": "freeform_text",
  "raw_input": "Family of 4 from Bangalore...",
  "structured_input": null,


  "expected": null,


  "system_output": {
    "nb01_packet": null,
    "nb02_decision_state": null,
    "nb02_hard_blockers": null,
    "nb03_behavior": null
  },


  "review": {
    "verdict": "usable",
    "failure_reason": "",
    "reviewer_notes": ""
  }
}
This lets you reuse your runner without much friction.


Minimum fields I’d insist on
For each real note, capture at least:


Input
id


source_type


raw_input


structured_input if any


optional context


Output
nb02_decision_state


nb02_hard_blockers


nb03_behavior summary


optionally full NB01/NB02/NB03 dumps


Review
verdict


failure_reason


reviewer_notes


That is enough to learn from.


Verdict categories
Use exactly these:


usable


needs_edit


wrong_direction


That is simple and good.


You can optionally add:


severity: low | medium | high


That helps later pattern analysis.


My recommendation
Tell the agent:


Yes, use the existing raw fixture format as the base for shadow mode.


But do not rely on the `expected` block for real notes.


For shadow mode:
1. keep raw_input / structured_input
2. add system_output
3. add review
4. set expected = null or omit it


The pipeline runner can stay the same, but shadow-mode records should clearly separate:
- input
- pipeline output
- human judgment
Bottom line
So:


yes, the format mostly exists


yes, the pipeline mostly exists


but shadow mode should use a slightly cleaner schema than standard fixtures


That is my call.

You’re thinking in the right direction, but you’re still framing this like “collecting data.”


This is not data collection.
This is extracting reality from operators.


If you just ask “send me notes,” you’ll get shallow inputs.
You need context + behavior + decision logic.


What you actually want from them
You need 3 layers:


1. Raw inputs (for shadow mode)
WhatsApp messages


Call summaries


CRM notes


2. What they did
What they replied


What they asked next


Whether they quoted / waited / dropped


3. Why they did it
What made them ask vs proceed


What signals they trust


What makes something “unsafe” to quote


Without layer 3, your system will look correct but behave wrong.


Who to reach out to (your 2 people)
Both are useful, but for different reasons:


1. Singapore agent (you booked with)
Strong for customer-facing behavior


Good for:


how they phrase responses


how they handle ambiguity


how they convert leads


2. Ex-vendor turned agency owner
Strong for decision logic


Good for:


how they decide to proceed vs ask


operational constraints


margins, feasibility, blockers


👉 You need both. Don’t pick one.


How to approach them (this matters a lot)
Do NOT say:


“I’m building an AI, send me data”


That triggers:


hesitation


filtered responses


low-quality samples


Instead:

“I’m trying to understand how top agents think and decide, especially from messy inputs”


Exact outreach message (use this)
Message
Hey — quick ask.


I’m working on something to understand how travel agents think when they get messy leads (WhatsApp, calls, half info).


Not building anything for you right now — just trying to learn how decisions actually happen.


Would love 30–45 mins with you. Specifically:


5–10 recent real inquiries (anonymized is fine)


what you replied


why you chose to ask vs quote vs drop


Goal is to understand how you decide:
“Do I proceed or do I need more info?”


Happy to share what I’m building once it’s clearer.


When are you free this week?


What to ask in the call (this is the core)
You’re not interviewing. You’re reverse-engineering their brain.


Part 1 — Raw reality
Ask:


“Show me the last 5 inquiries you handled”


“What did the customer actually send?”


Push for:


WhatsApp screenshots


messy text


partial info


Part 2 — Decision moment (most important)
For each one:


Ask:


“At this point, why didn’t you quote immediately?”


“What was missing?”


“What made this risky?”


You’re extracting:


blockers


hidden heuristics


risk signals


Part 3 — Question logic
Ask:


“Why did you ask these questions and not others?”


“Which questions actually change your decision?”


This maps directly to NB03.


Part 4 — Failure cases (goldmine)
Ask:


“Show me a case where things went wrong”


“When did you misjudge a customer?”


This exposes:


blind spots in your system


edge cases fixtures won’t capture


Part 5 — Fast heuristics
Ask:


“What do you look for in the first 30 seconds?”


“What makes you immediately cautious?”


These become:


derived signals


early routing logic


What you should capture (simple, not fancy)
For each case:


RAW:
Customer message


AGENT ACTION:
What they replied


DECISION:
ASK / PROCEED / DROP


WHY:
Reason in their own words


NOTES:
Anything interesting (contradiction, weird constraint, etc.)
That’s enough.


What you’re really doing (mental model)
You are building:


A decision system trained on operator judgment, not data


Fixtures = synthetic logic
Shadow mode = system validation
These calls = ground truth behavior


Common mistake (don’t do this)
Do NOT:


ask them to “send 30 notes”


ask structured questions only


rely on memory (“usually we do…”)


Always anchor on:


“Show me what actually happened last week”

If you do this poorly:


even 100 notes = useless

You’re not wrong. It is data.


The correction is about how you treat it, not what it is.


If you treat it as:


“collect data from agents”


you’ll get:


generic answers


polished examples


low-signal summaries


If you treat it as:


“extract real decisions from specific cases”


you’ll get:


messy inputs


real trade-offs


actual decision boundaries


Same thing physically. Different outcome.


On your India concern (this is real)
You’re right. Typical issues you’ll hit:


they won’t share real chats easily (privacy + habit)


they’ll summarize instead of showing


they’ll try to “look good” instead of being accurate


they’ll drift into storytelling instead of specifics


So design for that.


How to actually get good signal (practical tactics)
1. Lower the bar immediately
Don’t ask:


“Send me notes”


Ask:


“Can you just read out one recent inquiry and what you replied?”


Even a live walkthrough is enough.


2. Force specificity
Whenever they generalize:


“Usually we ask about budget and dates”


You respond:


“Okay, last message you got — what exactly did they say?”


Keep pulling back to one real instance.


3. Remove friction (critical in India context)
Say explicitly:


“Blur names, I don’t care about identity”


“Even rough or partial is fine”


“Voice note is fine”


The easier it is, the more real it gets.


4. Use your own booking as anchor
For the Singapore agent:


Say:


“When I came to you, what did you think was missing? Why didn’t you immediately quote?”


This is powerful because:


they remember it


no privacy issue


you get honest reasoning


5. Accept imperfect data
You will not get clean datasets.


You’ll get:


partial chats


recalled conversations


inconsistent detail


That’s fine.


You’re looking for:


patterns


decision triggers


failure cases


Not completeness.


Reframe your expectation
You don’t need:


30 perfect notes


You need:


~10 real situations where you understand:


input


action


reasoning


That’s enough to expose:


wrong decision states


weak question strategies


missing constraints


Blunt reality
70% of what they say = noise


30% = gold (specific cases, decisions, mistakes)


Your job is to dig until you hit the 30%


What I’d do if I were you
Given your constraints:


Start with Singapore agent (low friction)


Use your own trip as anchor


Extract 3–5 real cases


Then go to second guy with sharper questions


Bottom line
Yes, it’s data.


But if you approach it as “data collection,” you’ll get abstraction.


If you anchor everything to:


“show me exactly what happened”


you’ll get usable signal.


If you want, I can simulate:


a bad conversation vs a good one
so you see exactly how to steer them live without losing signal.




iare there ways to do this on a large scale like fb groups/cold mails etc?


Yes. But large-scale outreach for this is a funnel design problem, not just “send more messages.”


You’re trying to get one of three things:


interviews


real raw cases


ongoing pilot access


Each needs a different ask. Most people fail because they use one vague message for all three.


The channels that can work
1. Warm outreach
Highest signal, lowest scale.


This is still your best first move because:


trust is already present


privacy resistance is lower


they’ll actually show messy cases


Use this first for:


your Singapore agent


ex-vendor turned agency owner


referrals from them


Goal:


first 5 to 10 real cases


first 2 to 3 interviews


maybe first pilot


2. Facebook groups
Good for:


discovery


finding operators


lightweight polling


asking for interviews


Bad for:


getting raw customer chats publicly


deep trust fast


anything sensitive in public


Use groups to get:


DMs


interview volunteers


validation on pain points


Do not ask in a group:


send me your customer notes


Ask:


do you handle leads from WhatsApp and keep re-asking the same things?
would 20 mins help me understand how you currently do this?


3. Cold email / LinkedIn / WhatsApp outreach
Good for:


targeted interviews


pilot recruitment


building a pipeline


Bad for:


immediate deep sharing


cold asks for proprietary or private workflows


This works only if the ask is very narrow and credible.


4. Communities / associations
Travel associations, local operator communities, niche founder groups.


Good for:


credibility transfer


intros


maybe 1 webinar/demo later


Bad for:


immediate product feedback if you have nothing concrete to show


5. Landing page + form
Good for:


collecting interested agencies


separating serious from curious


giving people a low-friction next step


Bad for:


replacing direct outreach at this stage


This should support outreach, not replace it.


The scalable way to structure this
You need a ladder:


Stage 1: market conversations
Ask for:


15 to 20 minutes


no product pitch


understand workflow


CTA:


“open to a quick call?”


Stage 2: workflow review
Ask for:


one real example


anonymized if needed


walk through what they did


CTA:


“can you show one recent lead and how you handled it?”


Stage 3: shadow-mode collaborator
Ask for:


5 to 10 anonymized cases over time


feedback on your outputs


CTA:


“can I run your anonymized notes through the system and show you what it would have done?”


Stage 4: pilot
Ask for:


regular access


maybe one workflow


controlled use


CTA:


“want to test this on incoming leads for 2 weeks?”


Different ask at each stage. That is how it scales.


What to post in FB groups
Not:


“I’m building an AI travel agency copilot, who’s interested?”


Too broad, sounds generic.


Better:


“Travel agents handling leads on WhatsApp: what do you usually need before you can confidently send first options?”


“For small travel agencies, what usually causes back-and-forth before you can quote: dates, budget, destination clarity, passports, or group composition?”


“If someone messages ‘family trip, budget 3L, suggest options’ what are the first 3 things you must clarify before quoting?”


These do 3 things:


reveal real operator logic


attract the right people


create DM opportunities


Then DM the strong responders.


What to say in cold outreach
Short, specific, non-salesy.


Use this:


Message
Hi [Name] — I’m studying how small travel agencies handle messy inbound trip leads from WhatsApp/calls before they can confidently quote.


Not trying to sell you anything right now.


I’m looking to understand:


what info is usually missing


what makes you ask follow-ups vs proceed


where quotes or plans go wrong


Would you be open to a short 20-minute conversation? Even 1 or 2 anonymized real examples would help a lot.


For someone slightly warmer:


Message
Hi [Name] — quick one.


I’m trying to understand how travel agents actually think through messy trip inquiries, especially from WhatsApp and calls.


Would love 20 minutes with you sometime this week. If easier, even one recent anonymized inquiry plus what you asked next would be useful.


Not pitching anything right now — just trying to understand real workflow and decision logic.


What scales better than asking for “data”
Offer a tiny exchange.


Examples:


“I’ll summarize the patterns I see across agencies and share back.”


“I’ll turn one anonymized case into a structured decision flow and show you.”


“I’ll audit one inquiry thread and show where follow-up could be tighter.”


People respond better when they get something back.


The best lead magnet for agencies here
Not a generic AI pitch.


Use one of these:


1. “Inquiry-to-quote workflow review”
You review one anonymized lead and show:


what was missing


where time got wasted


what questions should have been asked first


2. “Quote/audit review”
For owners:


did agent quote too early?


were there missed blockers?


was there budget mismatch?


was there a safer question flow?


3. “Shadow co-pilot”
You run 3 to 5 anonymized leads through your system and compare with their real handling.


These are much easier to say yes to than “pilot my AI.”


What I would actually do if I were you
Week 1
Use warm outreach only.
Goal:


2 to 4 conversations


5 to 10 anonymized cases


refine your questions


Week 2
Start targeted cold outreach + FB/community posts.
Goal:


10 to 20 conversations booked


3 to 5 serious shadow-mode candidates


Week 3+
Pick 1 or 2 agencies for lightweight shadow mode.


Important caution
At scale, privacy and trust become the bottleneck.


So do this:


always say anonymized is fine


never ask for passport/payment info


accept paraphrased lead notes


ask for “what came in / what you asked / why” more than raw screenshots


That widens the pool.


My blunt view
Yes, FB groups and cold outreach can work.


But:


cold outreach gets conversations


trust gets real cases


real cases get pilots


Do not confuse the first with the second.


The highest-leverage path is:


warm intros first


use those insights to sharpen messaging


then do scaled outreach with a narrow ask


If you want, I’ll write:


one FB group post


one cold email


one LinkedIn DM
each tuned for this exact project.

You need variety in tone + specificity in ask, not just channel adaptation. Same message copy-pasted everywhere won’t work.


Below are 6 distinct outreach angles you can reuse across WhatsApp, email, LinkedIn, FB DMs.


Each has:


a different psychological hook


same underlying goal (get real cases + decision logic)


Version 1 — Direct, operator-to-operator (best overall)
Use for: WhatsApp, warm LinkedIn, referrals


Message
Hey — quick one.


I’m trying to understand how travel agents actually think when they get messy trip inquiries (WhatsApp, calls, half info).


Not building anything for you right now — just trying to learn how decisions actually happen.


Would love 20–30 mins with you. Specifically:


2–3 recent real inquiries (anonymized is fine)


what you asked next


why you chose to ask vs quote


Trying to understand: “when is it safe to proceed vs when do you need more info?”


Would you be open to this sometime this week?


Why it works:


low pressure


clear ask


grounded in reality


Version 2 — Pain-led (back-and-forth frustration)
Use for: FB groups, cold LinkedIn, email


Message
Hi — I’m studying how travel agents handle leads where customers send incomplete info like:


“family trip… budget 3–4L… suggest options”


What I’ve seen is a lot of back-and-forth before you can even start planning.


I’m trying to understand:


what info is usually missing


what makes you ask follow-ups vs proceed


where time gets wasted


Would you be open to a short 20-min chat? Even 1 recent anonymized example would help a lot.


Why it works:


mirrors their daily pain


makes them feel understood


easier to say yes


Version 3 — Specific scenario hook
Use for: FB posts, comments, cold outreach


Message

If someone messages you:
“Family trip, June/July, budget 4–5L, suggest options”

pulls in serious operators

Version 6 — Warm + personal (use for your 2 contacts)

Message

Why it works:


context-aware

very likely to convert


Where else you should look (beyond FB + cold outreach)
Think in terms of where operators already hang out, not where founders hang out.

multiple agencies


common mistakes


real patterns
