Part 6:


what happens for the gtm? how do we start reach outs, where, messaging? copies?etc?


I’m grounding this in your current product thesis and turning it into an actual first GTM motion, not generic “do content + sales” fluff. I’m checking current places where small travel agencies actually cluster, then I’ll give you the reach-out sequence, messaging angles, and starter copy.


I’ve got the channel map now. The useful part is clear: don’t lead with “AI travel OS.” Lead with one painful wedge, then expand after trust. The best real-world surfaces are associations/directories, trade events, LinkedIn, and travel-agent communities, not broad consumer-style marketing.


Thought for 1m 27s
Your GTM should start far narrower than the product.


Right now, the docs already point to the real wedge: this is not a consumer trip planner. It is a workflow compression tool for small travel agencies, especially the one-person or small-team operator living inside WhatsApp, Sheets, old packages, and memory. The docs also already define a strong lead-gen wedge: itinerary/quote audit, where you detect wasted spend, bad fit, missing cost buckets, and propose a smarter version. GTM in the project is still basically unstarted, while real-world validation and pricing are explicitly still open. 


 


 


 




So the first principle answer is this:


Do not start by selling the full “agency OS.”
Start by selling one painful, immediate outcome.


My pick:
Quote/itinerary audit for boutique leisure agencies
because it is easier to understand, easier to demo, and easier to say yes to than “replace your workflow with AI.” Your own product spec already frames this audit engine as both a lead-gen wedge and an agency-facing QA layer.


What you should sell first
Not “AI”
Not “agents”
Not “automation platform”


Sell this:


catch bad-fit quotes before they go out


reduce time from inquiry to first usable quote


stop junior mistakes


preserve agency knowledge when agents leave


improve margin discipline


Those pains are already concrete in your scenario set: bad quotes losing deals, knowledge walking out with staff, margin erosion, slow onboarding, and weak operational visibility.


Who to target first


Start with one ICP only:


Boutique outbound leisure agencies in India with 1 to 10 people, doing custom family, couple, or small-group trips.


Why this ICP:


they feel the pain hardest


decision-maker is reachable


they still run on WhatsApp + Sheets + memory


they can try a service wedge without long procurement


your scenarios and product thesis are already tuned to this exact operator profile


Who not to target first:


large TMCs


corporate travel-heavy players


pure ticketing shops


luxury-only agencies with highly bespoke white-glove service and low volume


consumers directly


Where to start reach-outs


Use a stacked channel approach.


1. Warm intros first
This is always the highest-conversion path. Ask founders, friends, parents’ network, startup operators, and anyone who has booked through travel agents in the last 12 months to introduce you to agency owners. No citation needed here because this is strategy, not fact.


2. Association directories and regional networks
TAAI says it has about 2,500 member companies and 20 regional units. IATO says it represents 2,000+ tourism industry members and has a public members list. These are useful because they give you concentrated agency/operator pools instead of random scraping. 


3. LinkedIn
Use owner/founder/director searches for “travel agency,” “tour operator,” “custom holidays,” “outbound travel,” “family vacations,” “honeymoon specialist,” especially in Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi, Ahmedabad, Kochi, Kolkata, Jaipur. This is where you do targeted founder-led outreach, not brand posting. General reasoning.


4. Trade communities
There are active Facebook communities for travel agents and operators, including groups explicitly for Indian travel agents/tour operators. These are useful for listening, soft posting, and recruiting interview calls, but they are bad for spammy pitching. 


5. Events
SATTE is a major annual B2B travel exhibition, OTM is positioned as a leading travel trade show, and TTF runs regional trade shows across India. These are high-density surfaces for customer discovery, demos, and pilot recruitment. 


What I would actually do first


I would not start with ads.
I would not start with a big landing page polish cycle.
I would not start with “content marketing” as the main motion.


I would do this instead:


Motion 1: Founder-led outbound + manual audit
You manually audit 10 to 20 real quotes/itineraries for agencies.
You show:


what was bad


what was missing


what could have been improved


how much time or margin they lost


This is the fastest path to signal because it tests pain, language, willingness to share data, and willingness to pay.


Motion 2: Discovery interviews disguised as teardown
Do not ask “would you use AI?”
Ask:


show me the last quote that got rejected


where do revisions start getting painful?


what goes wrong when a junior agent handles an inquiry?


what do you wish was remembered automatically?


Motion 3: Pilot offer
After 5 to 10 audits, sell a lightweight pilot:


X audits per month


quote QA


itinerary fit checking


maybe a live copilot for intake notes
Not full product. Just one workflow.


The offer hierarchy


Start with this ladder:


Free audit
Purpose: attention + trust + vocabulary learning


Paid done-for-you audit / quote QA
Purpose: validate willingness to pay before full product


Pilot
Purpose: embed into one agency’s workflow


Software
Purpose: scale only after repeated proven workflow


This matches your product wedge better than trying to jump straight to SaaS. Your docs already describe audit as the entry point and agency execution as the expansion path.


Messaging


Your messaging should not be product-first. It should be pain-first.


Three winning angles:


Angle A: Stop bad-fit quotes
For owners who worry their team sends the wrong thing.


Angle B: Faster first quote
For solo operators drowning in custom inquiries.


Angle C: Preserve agency knowledge
For agencies where everything sits in WhatsApp and one agent’s head.


The message formula:


Problem
You’re probably losing time/deals because quotes are built from old packages, memory, and scattered notes.


Proof
We audit a real recent quote and show budget mismatch, suitability misses, missing cost buckets, or wasted spend.


Offer
Send one recent itinerary/quote. I’ll return a marked-up audit and a smarter alternative.


CTA
Interested in trying this on one live case?


What not to say:


“AI-powered travel OS”


“multi-agent orchestration”


“knowledge graph”


“compiler pipeline”


“autonomous quoting”


That language is for your architecture, not the buyer.


Landing page headline options


Catch bad-fit travel quotes before they lose you the booking.


Turn messy inquiry notes into faster, sharper trip proposals.


The QA layer for boutique travel agencies: better-fit itineraries, fewer quote mistakes, faster turnaround.


Your agency’s memory and quote reviewer, built for WhatsApp-first travel teams.

Community post, not spammy

Why do some quotes get rejected even after a lot of work?

- budget mismatch
- wrong-fit hotel/activity choices
- missing inclusions/exclusions
- too many revisions before booking

What the first 30 days should look like


Week 1


make a list of 100 agencies


segment into solo / 2-10 staff / larger


identify 30 best-fit owner-led agencies


prepare one 1-page audit sample and one short Loom demo


Week 2


send 15 to 20 warm or semi-warm reach-outs


send 20 to 30 cold LinkedIn DMs


post one non-spam research post in 2 to 3 communities


book 5 to 8 discovery calls


Week 3


do manual audits for 3 to 5 agencies


capture repeated phrases they use


refine positioning based on what gets replies


push for 1 to 2 paid pilots, even if small


Week 4


productize the strongest wedge


make a tighter landing page around one outcome


turn the best audit into a case study


decide if the next motion is owner QA, junior agent guardrails, or itinerary audit


Metrics that matter early


Track only these:


reply rate by channel


% willing to share a real quote/itinerary


% willing to get on a call


% saying “this is a real problem”


% willing to pay for manual or pilot help


time to first pilot


Ignore vanity metrics for now.


My blunt recommendation


Start with:
founder-led outbound + free audit + paid pilot


Target:
boutique outbound leisure agencies


Lead with:
quote quality, fit, speed, and memory


Use channels in this order:
warm intros > LinkedIn > association/member directories > communities > events


Use events like SATTE, OTM, and TTF for concentrated discovery, not as your primary initial channel, unless you already have something demoable and a meeting plan. TAAI and IATO are useful because they concentrate relevant operators and member networks. 


The core mistake to avoid is trying to market the whole vision. The buyer does not buy “Agency OS.” They buy “help me not send bad quotes and waste half my day.”


I can turn this into a concrete outbound pack next: ICP list structure, lead spreadsheet columns, 3-offer ladder, and a full 2-week outreach sequence.

what would you like to look at next?


I’ve got enough from the tree and the project docs to narrow this. The next useful step is not more scenario reading. It is checking whether the repo already implements the compiler path cleanly from raw agency context to plan and prompt bundle.


Thought for 23s
Given your stated goal, I would look at this next, in this order:


notebooks/agency_context_to_plan_prompt_starter.ipynb
This is the most likely place where your actual target flow should live: agency owner context in, deconstruction, planning intent, then prompt bundle out. Your docs already converged on a compiler-style pipeline, not an open-ended “agent thinks” flow: normalize → validate → infer cautiously → decide → generate session strategy → generate prompt bundle. That pivot is explicit in the discussion log and first-mile plan. 


 




specs/context_deconstruction_pipeline.md
I want to see whether the notebook is grounded in a contract or whether it is drifting. The project docs are clear that raw context should become a canonical state packet first, with strict separation between facts, derived signals, hypotheses, and unknowns. If this spec is weak, the notebook will become prompt soup. 


 




specs/canonical_packet.schema.json and specs/source_envelope.schema.json
These matter before prompt quality. If provenance and field authority are loose, the rest is fake confidence. Your own decision policy says planning should only proceed once the Minimum Viable Brief is satisfied, otherwise ask follow-up, branch, or escalate. 




The actual pipeline code under src/pipelines/
Specifically I’d check whether these stages exist cleanly and separately:
normalize.py
validate.py
infer.py
decision.py or equivalent
session_strategy.py
prompt_bundle.py


Why this order: your repo already has plenty of scenario work, taxonomy, agent maps, and orchestration thinking. The missing risk is not lack of ideas. It is whether the first-mile execution path is actually deterministic and testable. The docs repeatedly push the same architecture: source adaptation, canonical normalization, validation against MVB, cautious inference, decision policy, session strategy, modular prompting, then execution. 


 


 




What I would specifically audit in that starter notebook:


Does it output a source envelope with provenance?


Does it produce a canonical packet with facts/signals/hypotheses/unknowns separated?


Does it run an MVB blocker check?


Does it produce a decision state: proceed, ask_followup, present_branches, or escalate?


Does it generate a session strategy before any prompt text?


Does it assemble a prompt bundle from blocks/registry, instead of free-writing a monolithic prompt?


If even one of those is missing, that is the next thing to fix. If all six are present, then we inspect whether the outputs are sharp enough on your agency-owner scenarios, especially quote disaster, knowledge-retention, and junior-agent guidance cases, because those are core product pressure tests. 


 


 




My choice: start with notebooks/agency_context_to_plan_prompt_starter.ipynb, then immediately compare it against specs/context_deconstruction_pipeline.md and the packet schemas. Paste that notebook here and I’ll review it against the contract, not vaguely.

Thought for 1m 3s
You’re right.


I should have remembered the current state is notebook-first, and that the src/pipelines path is still planned, not built. The first-mile doc explicitly says notebooks are the experimental lab before anything moves into src, and the goal right now is raw agency note → validated canonical packet → session strategy. 




I checked the starter notebook you uploaded. The important conclusion is this:


It is a useful pattern notebook, but it is not the travel notebook you need.


Why:


Your spec for this stage is travel-specific: raw agency note should become extracted travel state, bracket classifications, gap/contradiction analysis, and a voice-agent/session strategy. 




Your canonical schema is even stricter: it expects facts, derived_signals, hypotheses, unknowns, contradictions, blockers, decision_state, session_strategy, and optional prompt_bundle, all inside one CanonicalPacket.


But the starter notebook is generic business-context compilation. It uses fields like business_name, core_offer, target_audience, goals, deliverables, channels, and tools. That is not your travel intake model. It is a scaffold pattern, not the actual domain implementation.


So the next thing is not “look at another doc.”


The next thing is:


Convert the starter notebook into the actual Notebook 01→04 backbone for travel
Strong opinion: do not split into src yet, and do not add subagents yet. The compiler path in the technical scaffold is already clear: source adaptation → canonical normalization → validation → inference → decision → session strategy → modular prompting. 




What to do next, exactly
1. Keep the starter notebook, but treat it as a pattern only
Use it for structure:


dataclasses / typed objects


deterministic parse step


plan/session strategy step


prompt bundle step


validation cell


Do not keep its current field model.


2. Replace ContextIR with a travel-first intermediate or directly emit CanonicalPacket
Your schema already tells you the minimum useful truth model:


facts


derived_signals


hypotheses


unknowns


contradictions


blockers


decision_state


session_strategy


prompt_bundle


That means the notebook should stop thinking in:


business name


ICP


deliverables


channels


and start thinking in:


origin city


destination candidates


date window


duration nights


budget total


budget flexibility


party size / children / elderly


pace


trip style


mobility / medical / meal constraints


passport / visa status 




3. Make Notebook 01 deterministic and evidence-anchored
This is the real missing piece.


For each extracted fact, produce a FieldSlot with:


value


confidence


authority_level


extraction_mode


evidence_refs


updated_at 




Without that, you will not be able to separate:


what the agency explicitly said


what you inferred


what is missing


what is just a soft guess


That separation is the whole project’s backbone. It is repeated across the discussion log, the deconstruction spec, and the first-mile plan.


4. Notebook 02 should be pure blocker + contradiction logic
Not planning. Not prompting.


It should answer:


what is missing?


what is contradictory?


what is risky?


can we proceed?


Your schema and decision model already support this. Unknowns are tagged hard or soft, contradictions can be warning or blocking, and decision must resolve to one of:


ASK_FOLLOWUP


PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT


PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


BRANCH_OPTIONS


STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW


5. Notebook 03 should be cautious inference only
This is where you create:


derived_signals


hypotheses


Example:


fact: elderly present + relaxed pace


derived signal: mobility-sensitive routing


hypothesis: comfort-first mixed-age family


But do not leak hypotheses back into facts. Your whole compiler pivot was about preventing that failure mode. 




6. Notebook 04 should generate session strategy first, prompt bundle second
Your own spec says stage 4 is:


session goal


priority sequence


tonal guardrails


prompt profile for the voice agent 




So the order should be:


session goal


question order


must confirm


must not assume


tone


then prompt bundle


Not “prompt first and hope.”


The real diagnosis of the current starter notebook
What is good:


notebook-first mindset is correct


deterministic extraction before LLM is correct


plan and prompt separation is correct


validation cell exists


What is wrong for this project:


wrong domain model


wrong field vocabulary


no schema-level packet output


no explicit distinction between fact vs signal vs hypothesis vs assumption


no contradiction model


no decision-state engine


no source-envelope / evidence provenance at the packet level


So this notebook is not wasted. It is just one abstraction layer too generic.

Thought for 45s
Yes. That is the correct move.


Do not restart.
Do not jump to src yet.
Do not add more parallel notebooks unless a gap is truly missing.


You already have a real notebook spine. The right job now is to make it stricter, less duplicated, and more end-to-end trustworthy.


What I found
The current shape is:


NB01 exists and is doing the heavy lift of source envelope + extraction + normalization + derived signals.


NB02 is much cleaner contract-wise and has a clear decision-state machine.


NB03 is also fairly explicit and already includes mitigations for gaps found in NB02.


NB04/05/06 exist, but they still partly simulate NB01 instead of fully trusting the actual NB01 output.


The old starter notebook is a generic pattern scaffold, not the travel-domain source of truth.


So the project is not missing work. It is missing alignment.


The biggest problem right now
You have contract drift across layers.


Example:


The canonical schema speaks in terms like party_size, origin_city, destination_candidates, date_window, duration_nights, budget_total, pace, passport_status, etc. 




But NB02 contract uses blocker names like destination_city, travel_dates, traveler_count, selected_destinations, selected_itinerary, traveler_details. 




NB03 then inherits that language and sequencing logic. 




That means the architecture is conceptually right, but the interfaces are not yet stable.


If you do nothing else, fix this.


Strong recommendation
Keep the notebook chain.
But make these changes.


1. Make NB01 the single source of truth for packet shape
Right now NB01 is doing the most important work, but it is also the least maintainable part because it is essentially one huge implementation block.


That is fine for prototyping, but dangerous now.


Improve NB01 in-place
Not rewrite. Improve.


What to do:


Split the huge monolithic code cell into sections:


models


source adapters


normalizers


extraction


derived signal rules


unknown detection


serialization


tests/examples


Add an explicit final validation step against canonical_packet.schema.json


Add a “field coverage report” cell:


extracted fact fields


empty fields


unknowns


derived signals generated


evidence refs attached


Add 8-12 scenario-style raw notes directly in NB01 and verify extraction behavior


Why
Because everything downstream assumes packet integrity. That is the whole compiler pivot: normalize first, then validate, then infer cautiously, then decide, then generate strategy and prompts.


2. Unify vocabulary between schema, NB01, NB02, NB03
This is the highest-value cleanup.


Current risk
NB02 MVB blockers are using names that do not cleanly match the schema.
That makes testing look green while the actual interface is soft.


Fix
Create one canonical field map, either in NB01 or in a tiny shared helper notebook/module later:


Example:


traveler_count → party_size


travel_dates → date_window


destination_city → destination_candidates or a derived primary_destination


selected_destinations → branch/shortlist field


traveler_details → booking-stage structured fact group


Then rewrite NB02 blocker configs to use schema-aligned field names.


Because right now the contract and the schema are talking past each other.


My view: this is not optional.


3. Tighten NB02 around actual packet semantics, not abstract placeholders
NB02 is already conceptually solid:


blockers


contradictions


confidence


decision states


follow-up generation


branches


rationale 




But it needs two upgrades.


A. Use schema-level fields only
No mixed naming.


B. Separate these three things more sharply
missing


ambiguous


contradictory


Your own NB02 contract already admits one real limitation: ambiguous values like “Andaman or Sri Lanka” can slip through because field existence is being treated as sufficiency. 




That should not just be a note. It should become a first-class structure.


I would add one more packet/state concept:


ambiguities: list of fields that are filled syntactically but unresolved semantically


This prevents fake completeness.


4. Let NB03 consume NB02 cleanly, but stop patching too much there
NB03 is thoughtful. It already adds mitigations for:


ambiguity


urgency


budget stretch 




That is good short-term, but structurally two of those belong earlier.


My rule
If it affects truth of state, fix in NB01/NB02


If it affects how to ask next, fix in NB03


So:


Should move earlier
ambiguity detection


structured budget parsing


urgency signal extraction


Can remain in NB03
question sequencing


tone scaling


branch presentation


internal vs traveler-safe prompt boundaries


Otherwise NB03 becomes a patch layer for earlier notebook weaknesses.


5. Stop simulating NB01 in NB05 and NB06
This is the second major weakness.


From what I checked:


NB05 manually constructs packet pieces for the golden path


NB06 shadow runner also approximates or simulates NB01 extraction instead of depending on the actual NB01 output path


That is bad, because it can make the system look end-to-end when it is not.


Fix
Refactor NB05 and NB06 so they call the actual NB01 extraction path.


Even if that means:


importing executed code from NB01


or temporarily copying the exact NB01 extraction function into a shared helper


Ugly is fine for now. Fake end-to-end is not.


6. Make NB04 the enforcement layer, not just a runner
NB04 should become the answer to:
“Did the current compiler spine actually improve?”


Not just:
“Did it run?”


I would add these eval dimensions:


schema validity


blocker precision


contradiction recall


ambiguity detection rate


evidence coverage


decision-state correctness


question ordering correctness


traveler-safe boundary correctness


internal-draft assumption surfacing


That direction is already consistent with your broader evaluation framework and autoresearch path.


7. Treat the starter notebook as scrap metal, not foundation
Do not delete it yet.
Do not trust it either.


Use it only for reusable patterns:


deterministic parse before LLM


evidence objects


IR-to-plan separation


validation mindset


But it is not your travel compiler notebook. It is generic business-context scaffolding.


So the likely action is:


archive later


or pull a few useful ideas into NB01/NB03


but do not let it become a competing path


What I would change first, in order
Phase 1: Contract cleanup
Align field vocabulary between schema and NB02/NB03


Add ambiguity as first-class state


Add structured budget parsing in NB01


Add urgency signal in NB01


Phase 2: Spine hardening
Make NB05 use real NB01 output

Where each notebook should end up
NB01
Truth builder.
No prompting.
No session planning.
Only packet integrity.


NB02
Decision engine.
No traveler-facing language except follow-up question objects.


NB03
Turn planner and prompt assembler.
No repair of broken state except light conversational mitigation.


NB04
Regression and quality gate.

One honest end-to-end happy path using the real notebook chain.


NB06
One honest shadow-mode runner using the real notebook chain.


That is a clean system.

Thought for 1m 1s
Yes. But only a few, and they should answer product-contract questions, not write code.


Right now the risk is not implementation speed. It is building a neat compiler for the wrong slice of the business. The product thesis is not a consumer trip bot. It is a boutique travel agency OS that must preserve agency logic, sourcing hierarchy, operational fit, commercial fit, and audit/intelligence value.


So these are the only agent asks I want first:

Focus on:

Here’s the complete current state:


v0.1 Spine — Frozen
Notebooks (6)

NB01        Intake & Normalization        Defined
NB02        Gap & Decision        Defined + 81 tests passing
NB03        Session Strategy & Prompt Bundle        Defined + 15 tests passing
NB04        Eval Runner (2 modes)        31/31 fixtures pass
NB05        Golden Path Demo        Clean run end-to-end
NB06        Shadow Mode Runner        Ready, waiting for notes
Fixtures
Raw fixtures: 12 (3 clean, 3 messy, 2 hybrid, 2 contradiction, 2 branch)
Packet fixtures: 19 (8 ASK, 3 PROCEED_SAFE, 3 DRAFT, 2 BRANCH, 3 STOP)
Combined eval: 31/31 pass (100%)

Schema: Defined (data/shadow/SHADOW_MODE_SCHEMA.md)

Runner: Working (notebooks/06_shadow_runner.ipynb)

Contracts

NB04 (message generation) — ultra-narrow, only for PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE

Now I have the full picture. Here’s the analysis:


Top 15 Missing or Under-Modeled Concepts
Group A: Agency-Centric Context (these make it an agency OS, not a trip planner)
1. Agency Owner Seeded Context


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB02 (derived signal)
Type: Fact in NB01; derived signal in NB02
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: The current pipeline treats all explicit_owner facts the same. But an agency owner’s notes carry implicit context: “I know this family, they always overspend on hotels,” or “don’t suggest Supplier X — they burned us last time.” This is NOT the same as traveler-expressed preferences. It’s agency-owner tribal knowledge that should shape sourcing and risk flags but never appear in traveler-facing output.
Impact without it: The system can’t distinguish between “traveler said X” and “agency owner knows X is a problem.” Both land as explicit_owner authority.
2. Sourcing Hierarchy (Internal → Preferred → Network → Open Market)


Where: NB02 (derived signal) + NB03 (strategy item)
Type: Derived signal in NB02; strategy item in NB03
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: The MVB has routing_path in NB01’s classifier but it’s not a field in the MVB, and NB02’s decision engine doesn’t route based on it. The sourcing hierarchy is the agency’s competitive advantage — internal packages are cheapest and fastest, preferred suppliers have negotiated rates, open market is last resort. The decision state should influence sourcing path.
Impact without it: Every recommendation goes to open market. The agency’s relationships and negotiated deals are invisible.
3. Margin / Commercial Viability


Where: NB02 (derived signal)
Type: Derived signal
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: No field exists for margin, commission, or profitability. A trip that’s bookable but loses the agency money should be flagged. The current system has budget_conflict contradiction handling but it’s about budget-vs-luxury tension, not margin feasibility.
Impact without it: The system produces traveler-safe recommendations that are commercial suicide.
4. Partner Fit / Supplier Availability


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB02 (derived signal)
Type: Fact in NB01; derived signal in NB02
MVP: Later
Why missing: Does the agency have a preferred supplier for this destination? Do they have an internal package? This requires external supplier data that doesn’t exist yet. The slot can be added as a fact now and populated later.
Impact without it: System doesn’t know whether to route to internal packages vs open market.
Group B: Suitability and Risk (what the agent actually worries about)
5. Family/Toddler/Elderly Trip Suitability Score


Where: NB01 (derived signal) + NB02 (risk flag)
Type: Derived signal in NB01; risk flag in NB02
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: NB01 has traveler_composition as a fact but never derives “is this trip suitable for toddlers?” or “is this destination elderly-friendly?” The current TripComplexity classification in NB01 catches some of this but doesn’t produce a suitability score. NB02’s risk flags mention traveler-type risks but they’re hardcoded in the strategy builder, not derived from the packet.
Impact without it: The system knows “toddler present” but doesn’t flag “Maldives with toddler = long flight + no medical facilities = high risk.”
6. Document / Passport / Visa Risk


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB02 (hard blocker for booking, derived signal for earlier stages)
Type: Fact in NB01; hard blocker at booking stage; derived signal for discovery/shortlist
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: passport_status and visa_status are not in any MVB until booking stage. But for international trips, passport expiry should be flagged as a derived signal at discovery. If travel is in 3 weeks and destination requires visa, that’s a blocking issue from day one.
Impact without it: The system says PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE at discovery for an international trip where the family’s passports expire next month.
7. Booking Readiness Composite Score


Where: NB02 (derived signal)
Type: Derived signal
MVP: Later
Why missing: No single metric says “how ready is this trip to be booked.” The current system checks MVB per stage but doesn’t produce a composite score across all dimensions (documents, budget feasibility, traveler consensus, date certainty).
Impact without it: Agent can’t prioritize which leads are close to booking vs which need weeks of nurturing.
8. Budget Feasibility Check (Destination × Group Size × Budget)


Where: NB02 (derived signal / contradiction)
Type: Derived signal
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: The current budget_conflict contradiction only catches budget-vs-hotel-tier tension. It does NOT check whether the stated budget is feasible for the destination + group size at all. 50k/person for Maldives is impossible regardless of what hotel tier the traveler requests. This is a hard feasibility check, not a preference conflict.
Impact without it: The system proceeds with “PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE” for a budget that cannot possibly work, wasting the agent’s time.
Group C: Memory and History
9. Repeat Customer Memory


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB03 (strategy item)
Type: Fact in NB01; strategy item in NB03
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: No field for customer_id, is_repeat_customer, or prior_trips. The entire repeat-customer scenario (P1-S2) depends on recognizing the traveler and pulling their history. The current pipeline treats every intake as a fresh cold lead.
Impact without it: The system asks “what’s your budget?” to a customer who spent 3.2L on Dubai three months ago. The agent looks incompetent.
10. Prior Trip Preference Memory


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB03 (strategy item)
Type: Fact in NB01; strategy item in NB03
MVP: Later
Why missing: Related to #9 but more specific: what did they like/dislike on prior trips? “Loved Atlantis” → suggest water activities. “Complained about heat” → avoid SE Asia summer. This is enrichment data that doesn’t exist yet.
Impact without it: System can’t personalize suggestions based on actual behavior.
Group D: Audit and Intelligence
11. Audit Mode / Wasted-Spend Detection


Where: NB01 (fact) + NB02 (derived signal / contradiction)
Type: Fact in NB01; derived signal and contradiction type in NB02
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: The entire “audit” concept — upload a traveler’s self-planned itinerary and get a fit score + wasted-spend analysis — doesn’t exist in any notebook. The system has no concept of “what the traveler already planned” vs “what the agency would recommend.” This is the product’s wedge feature.
Impact without it: The system can only work forward (intake → plan). It can’t work backward (review existing plan → flag problems → suggest alternatives). This is the agency’s #1 trust-building tool.
12. Traveler Self-Plan vs Agency Recommendation Gap


Where: NB02 (contradiction type)
Type: New contradiction type
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: When a traveler arrives with their own plan (flights booked, hotel picked), the system should detect the gap between their choices and what the agency would recommend. This is a specific contradiction type: traveler_plan_vs_agency_recommendation. Currently the only contradiction types are field-level conflicts (dates, budget, destination).
Impact without it: The system treats a traveler’s pre-booked hotel the same as an unformed preference.
Group E: Boundary Enforcement
13. Internal-Draft vs Traveler-Safe Boundary Enforcement


Where: NB03 (constraint enforcement)
Type: Constraint / guardrail
MVP: Later
Why missing: The current boundary is soft — NB03 generates different prompts for internal vs traveler-safe, but there’s no programmatic gate that prevents leakage. The constraints list in PromptBundle is just text instructions to the LLM, not hard enforcement.
Impact without it: An LLM could accidentally include internal notes in traveler-facing output. The risk is real but low given the current design.
14. Risk Flags Per Traveler Type (Not Generic)


Where: NB03 (strategy item)
Type: Strategy item
MVP: MVP-now
Why missing: NB03’s SessionStrategyGenerator has hardcoded TRAVELER_RISKS dicts keyed by traveler type. But these are static strings, not derived from the actual packet. If a packet has destination_city: "Maldives" and traveler_composition: "family with toddler", the risk “flight timing with toddler” should be derived, not looked up from a static
The short version:


The spine works for generic trip planning. What’s missing are the concepts that make this an agency OS:


Priority        What’s Missing        Why It Matters
MVP-now (10)        Sourcing hierarchy, preferred suppliers, owner context, repeat memory, family suitability, internal/external boundary, budget feasibility, question quality, confidence precedence        These make the difference between “works” and “useful to a real agent”
Later (5)        Margin, document risk, audit mode, booking readiness, supplier tiers        Need pricing data, booking-stage implementation, or sourcing engine first
The biggest gap: NB02 doesn’t know about sourcing. A “Singapore, 3L, family of 4” query should produce a different sourcing path than “Singapore, 8L, honeymoon” — one might have an internal package, the other goes to preferred suppliers. The entire agency workflow depends on this.


Done. Two documents produced:


1. notebooks/15_MISSING_CONCEPTS.md — 15 concepts the product docs say should exist but don’t in the code. 10 are MVP-now (sourcing hierarchy, preferred suppliers, owner context, repeat memory, family suitability, budget feasibility, question quality, internal/external boundary, confidence precedence). 5 are later (margin, document risk, audit mode, booking readiness, supplier tiers).


2. notebooks/FIELD_DICTIONARY_AND_MIGRATION.md — Every field compared across schema (26 fields) vs NB02 MVB (15 fields) vs NB03 usage. Found 9 vocabulary drifts. The biggest:


Drift        Problem        Fix
destination_city (singular) vs destination_candidates (plural)        “Andaman or Sri Lanka” treated as resolved        Add destination_status (decided/semi-open/open)
travel_dates (fixed) vs date_window (flexible)        “March” and “March 15-20” look the same        Add date_confidence (fixed/window/unknown)
budget_range (one field) vs budget_total + budget_flexibility        “can stretch” signal buried in string        Split into two fields
traveler_count (one value) vs 5 structured fields        “big family” and “2 adults + 2 kids (8,12)” look the same        Add party_composition + structured breakdown
traveler_details (vague) vs passport_status + visa_status (specific)        Visa crisis can’t be modeled        Split into specific fields

Thought for 2m 49s
Agreed. No more “out of scope” as an escape hatch.


The right way to do this is:


Everything the travel-agency OS needs gets modeled now.
What can be phased later is only the execution layer, not the state model, decision logic, or handoff artifacts.


That is the clean line.


Also, no more agents are needed first. The two outputs you already got are enough to lock the direction:

it must separate internal draft from traveler-safe output


The governing rule for v0.2
Do not ask “is this in scope?”


Ask only:


Which layer owns this?


NB01 owns truth capture and normalization


NB02 owns judgment and routing


NB03 owns session behavior and prompt boundaries


NB04 owns evaluation


NB05 owns honest golden-path demos


NB06 owns honest shadow-mode replay


For P2 dashboards, CRM memory, post-booking anxiety, cancellation, emergency, referral, and review timing:


they are not excluded


they become either:


packet fields


derived signals


decision/risk modes


strategy profiles


or handoff artifacts


That is how you stay comprehensive without turning NB01–NB03 into a random monolith.


Full improvement plan

Make this first-class. Not hidden under unknowns.
Examples:

Risk flags

elderly mobility risk

margin risk

This is the main structural upgrade. It directly follows your missing-concepts audit and field-migration audit.


2. Stop treating “decision state” as the only axis
Keep the 5 decision states for spine stability:


ASK_FOLLOWUP


PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT


PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


BRANCH_OPTIONS


STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW


But add a second orthogonal axis:


operating_mode
normal_intake


audit


emergency


booking_readiness


follow_up


cancellation


post_trip


coordinator_group


owner_review


This solves your “no out of scope” requirement cleanly.


Example:


P2 quote disaster review is not “out of scope.” It is:


operating_mode = owner_review


likely decision_state = STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW or BRANCH_OPTIONS


S1 trip emergency is not “out of scope.” It is:


operating_mode = emergency


decision_state = STOP_NEEDS_REVIEW


ghost customer is:


operating_mode = follow_up


decision_state = BRANCH_OPTIONS or PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT


This lets the spine cover all 30 scenarios without inventing 20 new decision states.


That is the right architectural fix.


3. NB01 improvements
This is where the biggest work starts.


I inspected the notebook structure. NB01 is essentially one giant code cell with the right intent but too much packed into one place. That is fine for v0.1, but now it needs discipline.


NB01 must do these things in full
A. Replace legacy vocabulary
Your audit already found the main drifts:


destination_city → destination_candidates


travel_dates → date_window


budget_range → budget_total + budget_flexibility


traveler_count → party_size + structured composition


traveler_details → passport/visa/passenger-specific facts 




Do that now.


B. Parse ambiguity explicitly
If input says:


“Andaman or Sri Lanka”


“maybe Singapore”


“March or April”


“around 2L, can stretch”


“big family”


NB01 must not just store a string value.


It must emit:


normalized value


ambiguity marker


confidence


evidence refs


and a status field where needed


C. Parse agency-owner context separately
Do not bury owner knowledge inside generic facts.


Create:


agency_notes


owner_constraints


owner_priority_signals


Examples:


“never use Supplier X”


“client always overspends on hotels”


“this family hates long layovers”


“we have strong Sentosa package for this range”


These should shape routing and drafting, but never leak into traveler-facing output unless transformed into neutral, traveler-safe rationale.


D. Add repeat-customer hooks now
Even if the real CRM connector comes later, the packet must support:


customer_id


is_repeat_customer


past_trips


memory_hits


The external memory lookup can remain stubbed for now, but the packet structure must exist.


E. Add audit-mode intake now
NB01 must be able to ingest:


agency note


traveler freeform request


structured import


existing itinerary text


mixed package content


uploaded audit context


That means it must classify:


forward-planning intake


audit/review intake


from the start.


F. Add multi-party extraction now
For coordinator scenarios, extract:


main coordinator


sub-groups


per-group budget


per-group constraints


per-group payment posture


per-group missing docs


Even if partial, the structure must exist.


G. Add schema validation at the end
Every NB01 run should end with:


packet validity check


missing canonical slots report


ambiguity report


evidence coverage report


legacy alias warning report


This turns NB01 from “it ran” into “the packet is trustworthy.”


4. NB02 improvements
NB02 is conceptually the strongest notebook right now, but it is still too generic.


NB02 must become the real agency judgment engine
A. Add ambiguity as a blocker class
Not all filled fields are resolved fields.


This is one of your major gaps:
“Andaman or Sri Lanka” currently counts as destination-present unless patched later. Your own gap analysis says this is critical.


So NB02 must evaluate:


missing


ambiguous


contradictory


risky


infeasible


separately.


B. Add urgency-aware blocker logic
If urgency is high:


suppress low-value soft blockers


reorder questions


downgrade nice-to-have refinement


prioritize feasibility and documents


NB03 should not be patching this alone. NB02 must encode it.


C. Add budget feasibility enforcement
This is non-negotiable.


NB02 must not accept “budget present” as sufficient.
It must also ask:


is that budget feasible for this destination?


for this group size?


for this composition?


at this time window?


Even if initial feasibility is coarse and heuristic, the signal must exist now.


D. Add sourcing-path judgment
This is the biggest business gap.


NB02 must compute:


sourcing_path = internal_package | preferred_supplier | network | open_market


based on:


destination maturity


budget band


party composition


urgency


owner constraints


preferred supplier availability


repeat-customer pattern


operating mode


Without this, the system is not an agency OS. It is just a planner with paperwork.


E. Add suitability judgment
From packet facts, derive:


mixed-age suitability


toddler suitability


elderly suitability


mobility risk


medical risk


destination fit risk


Do not leave these as static NB03 strings.


F. Add document and visa judgment
Model it now, even if connectors come later:


passport valid / expiring / unknown


visa required / unknown / not required


travel-date-to-document timeline risk


This is already backed by real external systems. IATA’s Timatic is the industry-standard source for passport, visa, and health requirements, and Amadeus’ developer stack shows real travel APIs exist for flights, hotels, itinerary management, and related travel workflows. That is why these concepts belong in the model now, not as later abstractions. 


G. Add operating-mode-specific judgment
Per mode:


audit: compare traveler plan vs agency-fit signals


emergency: override normal follow-up behavior and create crisis-briefing outputs


follow_up: engagement and response gap signals


cancellation: policy path and alternative path generation


owner_review: flag commercial/suitability failures


coordinator_group: per-subgroup blocker logic


H. Add invariant rules
These should be hard assertions:


decision state is determined by blockers / contradictions / feasibility / mode, not by confidence alone


traveler-safe can never proceed if internal contradictions remain unresolved


hypotheses never fill hard blockers


derived signals can influence routing, but not silently override explicit facts


That last point matters because your own audit flagged confidence-precedence as a safety risk. 




5. NB03 improvements
NB03 should stop being a patch layer and become a policy-respecting conversation planner.


NB03 must do these things in full
A. Generate from decision_state + operating_mode + risk_flags
Not just decision state.


Same ASK_FOLLOWUP should feel different for:


vague family lead


last-minute visa risk


coordinator missing docs


audit-mode contradiction


repeat customer with partial memory


B. Replace static traveler-type risk text
Your own audit caught that current risks are too static. 




Generate risks from packet content:


toddler + late-night flight + long transfer


elderly + island destination + weak medical access


group disagreement + fixed budget


self-booked hotel + agency-fit mismatch


C. Add question intent
Every question should carry:


field asked


why this question matters


what downstream decision it unlocks


what assumptions it tests


That makes the agent feel supported instead of forced through a form.


D. Structural internal/traveler boundary
This is critical.


Do not rely only on prompt text saying “don’t mention contradictions.”


Make it structural:


every prompt block has audience = internal | traveler


traveler-safe builder receives only sanitized packet view


internal-only blocks are not even passed to traveler-safe renderers


add a sanitization pass that strips:


hypotheses


contradictions


blockers


unknown labels


owner-only notes


sourcing-path internals


commercial/margin notes


Then keep the golden-path leakage check on top of that.


E. Add mode-specific strategy builders
Need separate strategy profiles for:


normal intake


audit


emergency


follow-up


cancellation


post-trip review


coordinator group


owner review


Not separate notebooks. Separate builders.


F. Add branch-quality rules
BRANCH_OPTIONS should not just show alternatives.
It should distinguish:


branch because budget is flexible


branch because destination unresolved


branch because group priorities conflict


branch because traveler plan and agency fit diverge


These are different conversational jobs.


6. NB04 improvements
NB04 should become the hard gate.


Not just “31/31 fixtures passed.”
That is good, but too shallow for what you want.


Add these eval dimensions
schema validity


ambiguity detection precision/recall

packet


decision_result


session_strategy


prompt_bundle


traveler-safe sanitized output

8. NB06 improvements
Shadow mode should be honest, not postponed in practice.


You said it is “waiting for notes.” Fine. But design it so it can already replay:

That way, once real notes arrive, you are not redesigning the runner.

I’m not saying build those connectors now.
I’m saying the packet must already have homes for those truths.

Thought for 45s
Yes. I checked both docs.


Verdict: the direction is right. The v0.2 spec is substantially better than v0.1 because it stops pretending this is a generic trip planner and explicitly turns NB01 into the intake layer for an agency OS. The governing-principles doc is also right to force the “which layer owns this?” frame and to add the second axis for operating mode. 


 




But before implementation, I would lock 7 corrections. These are not optional polish. They prevent the next round of drift.


1. operating_mode should be top-level, not a fact
Your principles doc treats this as an orthogonal system axis, alongside decision state. That is correct. So do not bury it inside facts["intake_mode"]. Make it a top-level packet field such as operating_mode. Otherwise you mix traveler truth with system routing classification, and NB02/NB03 will keep treating it inconsistently. 




Use:


operating_mode at packet top level


keep decision_state at packet top level


keep traveler/owner/business data inside facts


2. Do not duplicate the same concept as both fact and derived signal
Right now the spec duplicates at least one important concept:


is_repeat_customer appears as a fact and also as a derived signal. 




That will rot quickly.


Use this rule:


fact = imported or explicitly known truth


derived signal = system inference or system-computed state


So:


customer_id = fact


past_trips = fact if imported, otherwise absent


is_repeat_customer = derived signal only


Same caution applies to anything like domestic_or_international, urgency, budget_feasibility, sourcing_path. Those should stay derived.


3. budget_total cannot stay a loose string if you want feasibility logic
The spec is right that v0.1 loses budget stretch/scope, but the replacement is still too soft. Examples like "400000", "4-5L", and "flexible" all under one field will break NB02 feasibility and branch logic.


Lock it as structured budget, even if extraction is imperfect:


budget_raw_text


budget_min


budget_max


budget_currency


budget_flexibility


budget_scope


Then feasibility can operate on numbers, not strings.


Same principle for date_window. A raw string is okay for storage, but add normalized fields:


date_start


date_end


date_confidence


Otherwise urgency and window-vs-fixed logic stays fragile.


4. destination_candidates is correct, but you still need one resolved planning target later
The rename from singular destination_city to plural destination_candidates is correct and necessary. It fixes the ambiguity problem your own audit identified.


But do not stop there. Add a downstream resolved field or explicit branch outcome:


resolved_destination


or branch-selected primary destination


Reason: shortlist/proposal/booking stages need a single active planning target, even if discovery starts plural.


So:


NB01 stores destination_candidates + destination_status


NB02 can keep ambiguity/blocker logic


later stage progression resolves to one destination


5. sub_groups needs a real object model, not a loose dict blob
The spec is right to model coordinator/multi-family flows now. Good. But sub_groups as a generic dict inside facts will get messy fast if you later need:


per-group payment status


per-group document completeness


per-group constraints


per-group contact/authority


per-group budget share


So define a SubGroup structure now, even if minimal:


group_id


label


size


composition


budget_share


payment_status


document_status


constraints


notes


That keeps NB02/NB03 usable for coordinator-group mode later.


6. agency_notes and owner_constraints need visibility semantics
The spec correctly introduces owner context as first-class. Good. But authority alone is not enough. You need explicit visibility/sanitization semantics attached to these fields, because they are exactly the kind of data that must not leak into traveler-safe outputs.


I would add one of:


visibility = internal_only | traveler_safe_transformable


or a per-field metadata rule in the packet schema


Examples:


“never use Supplier X” = internal_only


“family prefers shorter transfers” = traveler_safe_transformable


Without this, NB03 sanitization remains procedural instead of structural.


7. The schema, NB02 contract, and NB03 contract must be updated together, not sequentially
This is the biggest process risk.


Your field migration audit already showed major drift:


singular vs plural destination


travel dates vs date window


budget range vs total/flexibility


traveler count vs composition


vague traveler details vs passport/visa


no sourcing path concept 




So do not “finish NB01 first” in a vacuum. Lock three artifacts together in one pass:


canonical_packet.schema.json v0.2


NB02 field dictionary and blocker vocabulary


NB03 accepted input shape and sanitization rules


Otherwise you will just move the mismatch downstream again.


What is strong in the current v0.2 docs
These parts are right and should be kept:


first-class ambiguities


replacing legacy names with agency-OS names


owner/agency context as separate from traveler preferences


multi-party/coordinator modeling


audit-mode intake


urgency as a derived signal


budget feasibility as a derived signal


explicit schema validation at end of NB01


splitting the NB01 monolith into logical cells


the principle that modeling happens now and execution can phase later 


 




That is the correct foundation.


What I would lock immediately
Before code changes, freeze this contract set:


Top-level packet fields
packet_id


schema_version


stage


operating_mode


facts


derived_signals


ambiguities


hypotheses


unknowns


contradictions


blockers


decision_state


session_strategy


prompt_bundle


Facts only
Explicit or imported truths


Derived signals only
Computed routing/judgment values


Structural visibility
Especially for:


agency_notes


owner_constraints


owner_priority_signals


internal review content


Structured numeric/date normalization
Especially for:


budget


dates


urgency


feasibility


Bottom line
Your two new docs are good enough to move forward.
But I would not implement them exactly as written.


I would first revise them with these decisions:


rename intake_mode to top-level operating_mode

This is now materially better. At the doc level, the updated NB01 spec and governing-principles doc absorb the seven corrections cleanly: operating_mode is top-level, fact vs derived duplication is removed, budget and dates are structured, destination progression is explicit, SubGroup is structural, owner data gets visibility semantics, and NB01 is explicitly co-locked with NB02/NB03 instead of drifting alone. 


 




My verdict is:


Yes, this is now good enough to treat as the v0.2 design baseline.
But not yet good enough to implement blindly. There are still a few contract-level mismatches to fix first.


What is now correct
The architecture is finally talking like an agency OS, not a generic planner. That is obvious from the top-level packet shape, owner-context fields, audit/coordination support, and the two-axis model of decision_state plus operating_mode. 


 




The separation of concerns is much cleaner:


NB01 captures truth and normalization


NB02 judges


NB03 plans the next interaction


later systems consume handoff artifacts, instead of being hand-waved away as “out of scope.” 




The biggest structural upgrades are now right:

What still needs one more correction pass

In the updated spec, v0.2 budget is budget_raw_text + budget_min + budget_max + budget_flexibility + budget_scope, but the compatibility snippet still maps budget_range to budget_total. That field no longer exists in the updated NB01 spec. Fix that now or the migration layer will lie. 




The actual schema file is still old.
Your live canonical schema still reflects the older packet contract with schema_version: "0.1" and the older shape for facts, derived signals, hypotheses, blockers, and top-level fields. The new NB01 spec is much better, but until canonical_packet.schema.json is rewritten, you still have two truths. 


 




agency_notes and owner_priority_signals still need explicit visibility treatment, not just owner_constraints.
You fixed visibility semantics for OwnerConstraint, which is good. But the same leakage risk exists for agency_notes and likely parts of owner_priority_signals. If they are truly internal, mark them explicitly as internal-only or define sanitization rules at schema level. Right now only one owner-context type is structurally protected. 




The discovery MVB in validation still uses budget_raw_text, not resolved numeric budget.
That may be fine, but it needs an explicit rule. Decide one of these:


raw budget text is enough to proceed at discovery, numeric parsing is optional


or numeric parse success is required for certain modes like audit, feasibility-heavy, or international family trips.
Right now the validation logic implies the first, while the rest of the spec leans toward stronger numerical reasoning. 




The urgency test example is date-fragile.
The test says "2026-03-15" (7 days from now) should produce high urgency. That is already false relative to the current date. Make the test relative to execution date, not tied to a hardcoded calendar date. This is small, but it is exactly the kind of thing that silently rots test suites. 




resolved_destination exists only as downstream prose, not yet as a shared locked contract item.
The NB01 spec correctly says NB01 never sets it, and NB02 introduces it later. Good. But because it is important for shortlist/proposal/booking, it should be formalized in the shared schema/NB02 contract, not left as a side note.

Thought for 57s
This is close. Much closer.


My read after checking the locked artifacts together is:


You now have a credible v0.2 contract set.
But it is not fully clean yet. There are still a few contract mismatches and implementation traps that will bite you if you start coding immediately.


So the right answer is not “looks good.”
It is:


Lock 6 more fixes, then implement.


What is now genuinely solid
These parts are now aligned enough to trust:


top-level operating_mode


no fact/derived duplication on repeat-customer status


structured budget and date normalization


first-class ambiguities


SubGroup as a structural type


owner visibility semantics


NB01 / NB02 / NB03 being co-locked instead of drifting independently 


 


 


 


 




That is the first time the system feels like a real agency-OS contract instead of layered notes.


The 6 fixes still needed before implementation
1. resolved_destination is still not actually locked
You said it is formalized as a shared contract item. But in the actual schema, I do not see resolved_destination anywhere, and in NB02 I do not see it in the MVB field inventory either. NB01 and the principles doc talk about downstream resolution, but the shared schema and NB02 contract still stop at destination_candidates.


That matters because:


shortlist/proposal/booking need one active planning target


sourcing-path logic becomes fuzzy without a resolved target


branch resolution has nowhere canonical to land


Fix
Add resolved_destination explicitly to the shared schema and to the NB02 stage-specific contract, probably as:


soft in discovery


hard in shortlist onward


Without that, “destination progression” is still mostly prose.


2. Schema and NB02 still disagree on later-stage facts
The schema v0.2 is cleaner, but it does not include some things NB02 expects at later stages:


selected_itinerary


payment_method


possibly explicit booking-passenger fields beyond passport_status / visa_status
NB02 uses them in proposal/booking hard blockers, but the schema’s FactsMap does not define them.


That is a real tri-contract mismatch.


Fix
Either:


add those fields to the schema now, or


remove them from NB02 and replace them with schema-native equivalents


My recommendation: add them now. They are not optional if proposal/booking are real stages.


3. The schema still does not strongly type field values enough
Right now FieldSlot.value is basically unconstrained JSON, and every field in FactsMap points to the same generic FieldSlot. That means the schema is only validating the shell of the slot, not the real shape of important values like:


owner_constraints as list[OwnerConstraint]


sub_groups as dict[str, SubGroup]


passport_status as a structured per-traveler map


visa_status as a destination/timeline/status object 




So the schema is better than before, but still too weak to prevent malformed packets.


Fix
For the critical structured fields, define field-specific value shapes, for example:


owner_constraints → FieldSlot whose value is array of OwnerConstraint


sub_groups → FieldSlot whose value is object with SubGroup values


passport_status → FieldSlot whose value matches a defined status map


visa_status → FieldSlot whose value matches a defined visa object


Right now the schema reads like a contract, but still behaves partly like a loose container.


4. NB03 sanitization is too aggressive and too narrow at the same time
This is the most subtle issue.


NB03’s sanitize_for_traveler() removes:


all hypotheses


all contradictions


all unknowns


all ambiguities


all derived signals


owner-only fields 




That is safe, but too blunt.


Problem:


some derived signals are exactly what a traveler-safe conversation may need in transformed form:


budget_feasibility


urgency


maybe even value_gap in audit mode


meanwhile the code example only explicitly filters agency_notes, owner_priority_signals, and internal-only owner_constraints, but your broader visibility semantics apply to more than that.


So the sanitization model is not yet mature enough.


Fix
Move from “drop everything derived/internal” to:


an allowlist of traveler-safe transformed fields/signals


a denylist of internal-only fields


mode-specific traveler-safe transforms


Example:


budget_feasibility = infeasible should not leak internal jargon, but it should enable traveler-safe phrasing like “At the current budget, this destination may be difficult without trade-offs.”


urgency = high should drive shorter questioning, even if not directly shown


The current model is safe, but it risks crippling NB03’s usefulness.


5. NB03 code examples still contain vocabulary/shape drift
I found at least two concrete mismatches in the NB03 spec examples:


A. owner_notes vs agency_notes
The dynamic risk-generation example checks packet.facts.get("owner_notes"), but the shared schema and NB01 spec use agency_notes.


B. sub_groups treated like dicts, not SubGroup
The example uses g.get("budget_share", 0) inside groups.values(), but your v0.2 design made SubGroup a structural type. That means access should be attribute-based or standardized serialized-object access, not loose dict access.


These are exactly the kinds of mismatches that create silent drift between the contract and notebook code.


Fix
Run one consistency sweep through all example code blocks and rename/access fields to match the locked contract literally.


6. The external-rail assumptions are right, but they imply one more design constraint
Your architecture is still sensible relative to live industry rails:


IATA Timatic remains a real, current source for passport, visa, and health requirement checking. 


Amadeus Self-Service still exposes flights, hotels, destination experiences, cars/transfers, market insights, and itinerary-management APIs. But its self-service catalog has real limitations: it returns published GDS rates, excludes some carriers, supports only up to 9 passengers per booking, and ticket issuance requires enterprise/consolidator paths rather than simple self-service ticketing. 


That means your contract should explicitly distinguish:


advisory/truth-layer signals from public/self-service data


agency-specific commercial truths like negotiated rates, supplier preference, margin targets, special inventory access


Otherwise later you will accidentally treat self-service/public travel data as equivalent to agency-owned commercial sourcing truth.


Fix
In the model or governance docs, define a sourcing-authority hierarchy:


public/self-service market data


preferred/network/internal supplier data


owner override / agency operational knowledge


This fits your product thesis and avoids future authority inversion.


My overall verdict
What is done
You now have:


the right conceptual spine


the right top-level packet philosophy


the right two-axis model


the right direction for ambiguity, visibility, and structural typing


What is not done
You do not yet have a fully implementation-clean contract set, because:


later-stage fields are not fully aligned


resolved_destination is not truly locked


schema typing is still too loose for key structured values


NB03 sanitization and examples still drift


What I would do next, exactly
Before code:


Pass A: final contract cleanup
Add resolved_destination to shared contract


Add selected_itinerary and payment_method to schema, or remove/replace them in NB02


Strongly type critical FieldSlot.value payloads


Rewrite NB03 sanitization as allowlist/denylist with transforms


Fix NB03 example vocabulary/shape drift


Add sourcing-authority hierarchy note to governance or schema commentary


Pass B: implementation begins
Then start NB01.


Not before.


Bottom line
This is no longer “design exploration.”
This is now pre-implementation contract refinement.


You are one cleanup pass away from a real build.


If you want the blunt answer:


Do not write notebook code yet.
Do one last contract pass, then implement NB01.




the agenst own assessment:Honestly? Yes, the direction is right. But there are real risks I should name.


What’s structurally correct now:


The layer ownership (NB01=truth, NB02=judgment, NB03=conversation) prevents monolith rot. This is the single most important fix over v0.1.


The two-axis model (decision_state × operating_mode) is the right answer to “how do we cover 30 scenarios without 30 decision states.”


Making ambiguity first-class, budget/dates numeric, owner data visibility-qualified — these are all necessary, not optional. The v0.1 schema was a generic trip planner. The v0.2 schema is an agency OS.


What I’m less confident about:


Extraction reality gap. The schema now has 30+ fact fields, structured composition, ambiguity detection, owner constraints with visibility, multi-party subgroups. The actual extraction layer is keyword-based mock code in a single function. The gap between “this field exists in the schema” and “the LLM reliably extracts it from ‘family of 5, Europe, June/July, 4-5L, kids want snow, parents can’t walk much’” is the real engineering challenge. We’ve designed the destination without the road.


Notebook-as-architecture has a ceiling. Splitting one 881-line cell into 12 cells is better but doesn’t change the medium. This is becoming a real system with contracts, invariants, and downstream consumers. At v0.3, the core should be a Python package with notebooks as thin exploration/demo wrappers.


Stubbed logic that looks real. sourcing_path defaults to "open_market" with a comment saying “will be enriched.” budget_feasibility needs a min-cost table that doesn’t exist. These are honest stubs, but they create the illusion of capability without the engine. The risk is that downstream code starts depending on signals that are just placeholder strings.


Migration blast radius. Six field renames across fixtures, tests, NB02 MVB, NB03 question templates, and golden path demos. The LEGACY_ALIASES layer in NB02 is the right mitigation, but it’s debt.


What I’d do differently if starting today:


Write the 13 NB01 tests before the implementation. Make them fail. That locks the contract before code drifts it.
Upgrade the mock extraction from keyword matching to at least regex+pattern-based, so the tests exercise real extraction paths.
Mark every derived signal clearly as “stub” or “live” so nothing downstream silently depends on a placeholder.
Bottom line: The architecture is sound. The model is right. The execution risk is entirely in the extraction layer — everything else is plumbing.


Thought for 19s
Mostly right. Not fully.


I’d rate that assessment 80 percent correct.


What it gets right:


The layer split is the right backbone now. NB01 owns truth capture, NB02 owns judgment, NB03 owns conversation planning, and the two-axis model of decision_state × operating_mode is the right way to cover many scenario families without exploding the state machine. That is now explicit in your governing principles and the locked specs.


The schema shift is also real progress. v0.2 is no longer a generic trip-planner packet. Top-level operating_mode, first-class ambiguities, structured budget/date fields, owner visibility semantics, and SubGroup all push it toward an agency OS.


The biggest warning is also valid: there is a large gap between “field exists in schema” and “field is reliably extracted from messy owner notes.” Your own NB01 spec still says the current state is one giant code cell with mock LLM and lightweight rules, and the v0.2 spec adds a lot more surface area. That is the hard engineering part. 




Where I disagree with the agent:


It says “everything else is plumbing.” That is too optimistic. The risk is not only extraction. There are three co-equal risks now:


Extraction reality gap
Yes, this is the biggest one.


Stubbed judgment signals becoming fake truth
sourcing_path, budget_feasibility, preferred_supplier_available, and related signals can easily look authoritative while still being placeholder heuristics. Your own specs explicitly describe some of these as stubs or dependent on external tables. If downstream notebooks start trusting them too early, the system will feel more mature than it is.


Contract drift during migration
You renamed core fields, added later-stage concepts, introduced operating_mode, and locked tri-contract updates for exactly this reason. That means migration blast radius is not just “debt.” It is a real failure mode. If examples, tests, fixtures, and schema drift, the architecture will rot even with good extraction.


On “notebook-as-architecture has a ceiling”:


Yes, true. But not the next problem.


The wrong move now would be to jump into a Python package before the v0.2 contracts and test matrix are actually proven. The right sequence is:


lock contract


write failing tests


make NB01/NB02/NB03 pass honestly


only then extract stable core into a package


If you package too early, you will freeze the wrong abstractions. The governing principles already imply this sequencing, even if they do not say it outright.


What I would change in the agent’s “what I’d do differently” section:


Writing the 13 NB01 tests first is correct.
Upgrading from keyword matching to regex/pattern-based extraction is also correct.
Marking derived signals as stub vs live is necessary.


But I would add one more rule:


No downstream notebook may rely on a stubbed derived signal unless the signal carries an explicit maturity tag.


Something like:


maturity = stub | heuristic | verified


or signal_source = placeholder | rules | external_data


Without that, you will eventually build polished behavior on top of placeholder signals.


My bottom line:


The architecture is now sound enough.
The model is mostly right.
The execution risk is primarily in extraction, but secondarily in fake-confidence signals and migration drift.


So the next move is not more design discussion.


It is:


write the failing NB01 v0.2 tests first


implement only enough extraction/normalization to pass them honestly


tag every derived signal as stub/heuristic/live


keep NB02/NB03 conservative wherever signals are still stubbed


That is the path that prevents self-deception.
