Thought for 6s
Yes. The order is still:


NB01 → review → NB02 → review → NB03 → review → then UI/flow simulation.


Not because UI is unimportant.
Because the UI should sit on a spine that is already honest.


If you do UI earlier, two bad things happen:


you hide logic problems behind a nice surface,


you end up rewriting the UI as NB02/NB03 contracts change.


The exact order I’d use now
1. NB01
Done enough to proceed.


2. NB02
Implement and review.


Why now:


this is where ambiguity, feasibility, urgency, contradictions, and mode routing become real


if this layer is weak, any UI will just visualize bad judgment


3. NB03
Implement and review.


Why before UI:


the UI needs to display:


internal draft


traveler-safe preview


follow-up sequence


branch prompts


safety/leakage status


all of that depends on NB03 being real, not mocked


4. Then build the UI
At that point the UI becomes valuable instead of premature.


And the UI should be exactly what we discussed:


one internal app


two modes:


Workbench Mode


Flow Simulation Mode


Why not UI right after NB02?
Because after NB02 you still won’t have the real conversational layer.


You would only have:


packet


blockers


decision state


rationale


That is useful for debugging, but not enough for the actual product-flow simulation we discussed.


The product-flow simulator only becomes worth building after NB03, because then you can show:


what the agency operator sees


what the system recommends next


what traveler-safe output would look like


what gets sanitized away


how branch options are framed


That is the first point where the UI represents the real system instead of a placeholder.


So the clean sequence is
NB01 patch/accept
→ NB02 implement
→ NB02 review
→ NB03 implement
→ NB03 review
→ UI app
   - Workbench Mode
   - Flow Simulation Mode
→ then broader eval / shadow / golden-path integration
What to avoid
Do not let the agent:


jump to UI after NB02


start FastAPI/Flask/prod infra after NB02


rename packages again during NB02/NB03


create a fake chat interface before NB03 exists


Strong opinion
The UI should come after NB03, not after NB02.


Because before NB03, you are still testing judgment.
After NB03, you can test experience.


That is the right order.

Bug Fixed During Implementation
Decision state fallthrough: The soft-blocker check was NOT inside an else: branch of the main if/elif chain. After elif hard_blockers: correctly set decision_state = "ASK_FOLLOWUP", the code fell through to the soft-blocker block which overwrote it to PROCEED_INTERNAL_DRAFT. Fixed by adding else: to close the chain.

Thought for 38s
My read from the actual NB02 code and tests:


Patch current implementation before NB03.


It is close, but not clean enough to advance as-is.


The good news is the problems are not “rewrite everything” problems. They are fixable contract and test-strength issues.


Overall verdict
The NB02 implementation is directionally solid. It is using the real NB01 packet path, the decision engine is shared/importable, ambiguity classification exists, urgency suppression exists, budget feasibility exists, and the fallthrough bug was a real catch.


But I would not call it fully honest enough for NB03 yet, because there are still a few places where:


the code drifts from the intended contract,


the tests are weaker than the summary suggests,


and some important stage semantics are not implemented correctly.


What is good
Real shared decision module exists


src/intake/decision.py is an actual shared layer, not notebook-only logic. 




The engine uses the real NB01 extraction path in tests


The tests mostly go through ExtractionPipeline().extract() -> run_gap_and_decision(), which is the correct shape. 




Important invariants are present


blocking ambiguities prevent PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


infeasible budget prevents PROCEED_TRAVELER_SAFE


hypotheses do not fill hard blockers. 




The fallthrough bug fix mattered


That bug could have made the entire decision engine look cleaner than it really was. Fixing it was necessary. 




Critical issues
1. Shortlist/proposal stage contract drift
The biggest real issue in the code is that later-stage routing is still not aligned with the intended contract.


MVB_BY_STAGE["shortlist"] still uses destination_candidates twice and never actually requires resolved_destination. Proposal/booking also still key off destination_candidates rather than a single resolved planning target. 




That matters because your whole earlier contract cleanup explicitly moved toward:


destination_candidates in discovery


resolved_destination downstream


Right now the code has the field in schema, but the decision logic is not truly using it.


This is not minor. It means stage progression is still partly fake.


2. Tests are weaker than the summary claims
The implementation summary says all required NB02 behavior is proven. That overstates it.


Examples from the actual test file:


the urgency suppression test does not really prove soft-blocker suppression robustly; it mostly checks that some hard blockers are not present. 




the infeasible-budget test allows "infeasible", "tight", or "unknown", which makes it much easier to pass without actually proving the intended behavior. 




coordinator-group, owner-review, and audit tests mostly prove mode detection, not the actual mode-specific judgment behavior. 




contradiction precedence still relies on manual contradiction injection because NB01 does not generate contradictions automatically yet. That is acceptable as an isolated test, but it means the full path is not proven. 




So “14 tests pass” is true.
But “NB02 contract is fully proven” is not.


Medium issues
1. Budget feasibility is still too eager for ambiguous destinations
check_budget_feasibility() uses the first destination candidate when multiple candidates exist. 




That can be okay as a heuristic, but when destination ambiguity is unresolved, feasibility should either:


stay conservative,


or explicitly mark itself as ambiguity-sensitive,


not quietly behave as if the first candidate is the true one.


2. internal_data_present / leakage risk coupling is still narrow
The decision engine only raises traveler-safe leakage risk when packet.hypotheses or packet.contradictions exist and internal_data_present is set. 




Given your earlier concern, this may be too narrow if internal-only owner fields or ambiguity-heavy packets also matter for later sanitization behavior.


3. Package naming debt remains
I still do not like src/intake/ as the long-term package identity. It is acceptable to continue for now, but it is still project-structure debt, not a clean end-state.


False-confidence risks
1. The summary claims more than the tests prove
The implementation summary says things like “all 11 required NB02 tests pass” and “ready for NB03.” The actual tests do not yet prove the stronger stage semantics and several mode behaviors deeply enough.


2. Feasibility maturity is present, but not yet operational enough
The heuristic budget table is okay for now, and the code does not wildly overtrust it. Good. But tests do not yet force the engine to demonstrate clearly different behavior for:


verified-like hard evidence,


heuristic evidence,


unknown feasibility.


So the maturity discipline exists, but it is not tested hard enough.


Exact fixes before NB03
Fix downstream stage semantics


Use resolved_destination properly in shortlist/proposal/booking logic.


Do not keep relying on destination_candidates as the downstream active target. 




Strengthen the weak NB02 tests
Add or tighten tests for:


urgency suppression that explicitly checks which soft blockers remain


infeasible budget that must actually be infeasible, not unknown


shortlist requiring resolved destination


audit mode doing audit-specific judgment, not just mode detection


owner_review doing commercial/risk-focused judgment, not just mode detection


coordinator mode using subgroup-derived readiness or subgroup-specific blockers, not just detecting the mode


Make feasibility conservative under unresolved destination ambiguity


either skip hard feasibility when destination is unresolved,


or mark the result explicitly as ambiguity-sensitive and keep it advisory until destination resolves


Add at least one stage-progression test


discovery packet


shortlist packet with resolved destination


proposal packet with selected itinerary


booking packet with documents/payment


prove the engine actually changes behavior by stage

Part 8:


how do we test and break scenarios, the whole process etc? look at all conversations, think and then lets discuss


I’m pulling the testing spine out of the scenario docs and the compiler-pipeline design, then I’ll turn it into one practical test process instead of scattered ideas.


One useful pattern is already clear: your docs are strongest when they start from a real failure, not a function. So the test unit is not “does parser return JSON,” it is “does the system stop Anita from sending a Thailand quote with the wrong visa assumption.”


Thought for 2m 13s
I went through the project thread and the docs. The shape is clear now.


You should test this as a compiler pipeline, not as a vague “AI agent.” The core move in your docs was already the right one: raw context gets normalized into a canonical packet, then validated, then cautiously inferred, then a decision state is chosen, then the next question or internal plan is generated. Testing has to follow that same chain, otherwise you end up testing prose quality while the real logic stays unguarded. 


 


 




The right testing hierarchy is:


Scenario tests, based on real messy work.


Pipeline tests, for Notebook 01 and Notebook 02 outputs.


Code tests, for functions and components.


Offline evals, for prompt packs and routing changes.


Shadow mode and pilot validation, before trusting production. 


 


 




That order matters. If you start from unit tests only, you will get green checks on useless logic. Your own documentation already says persona scenarios define what to build, and code tests only validate implementation details. That is exactly right. 




What to test
Test every scenario across these five layers.


1. Input understanding
Given raw note, WhatsApp, voice transcript, or uploaded plan, did Notebook 01 extract the right facts, preserve provenance, separate facts from derived signals, keep hypotheses soft, and mark unknowns instead of inventing? That is the whole canonical packet idea. 


 




2. Decision correctness
Given that packet, did Notebook 02 choose the correct state: PROCEED, ASK_FOLLOWUP, PRESENT_BRANCHES, or ESCALATE? The MVB blocker logic is central here. Origin, dates, budget, and traveler composition are the hard minimum. 


 




3. Question quality
If the state is ASK_FOLLOWUP, are the questions minimal, ordered, and stage-aware? Your session strategy rules matter here: do not ask everything at once, do not ask generic questions when you already have hypothesis hints, and do not ask more than three questions at a time. 




4. Commercial and operational sanity
Even if the extraction and decision are correct, the system can still be wrong in agency terms. It must respect package-first and preferred-supplier-first sourcing, protect margin, catch wasted spend, and consider operational fit, not only traveler fit. That is a separate axis of correctness. 


 


 




5. Trust and failure visibility
The product dies on silent errors, not just wrong answers. A visible “I’m unsure” is survivable. A confident bad move is not. Your other work captured this well: false positives, false negatives, silent failure, and confidence betrayal are the killers. Same principle applies here. 


 




How to break it
This is the more important part.


Do not only test golden paths like “family of 4, Singapore, 3L, dates known.” Those prove almost nothing. Break it by attacking the exact failure modes your system is vulnerable to.


A. Missing blocker attacks
Feed cases where one hard blocker is absent but everything else looks rich. Example: great family detail, clear budget, clear destination vibe, but no travel window. The system must resist the temptation to proceed. 




B. Contradiction attacks
Examples:


“Luxury stay” plus “2L total budget for Europe family trip”


“Relaxed pace” plus “3 cities in 4 days”


“Toddler and elderly” plus “theme-park-heavy packed days”


“Business class, 5-star, flexible dates” but then “strictly budget-first”
These should not become polite generic follow-ups. They should trigger contradiction flags and possibly escalation or branch presentation. 


 


 




C. Authority inversion attacks
Give conflicting sources:


agency note says budget 5L


customer later says 3L hard cap


old customer history suggests premium behavior
Which source wins? If your system quietly trusts stale memory over explicit current input, it will be wrong in a dangerous way. Your docs call for preserving provenance. Test that hard. 


 




D. Stage blindness attacks
The same fact should produce different outputs depending on stage. Missing passport validity at discovery is a follow-up. Missing passport validity two weeks before departure is a stop and crisis path. The solo and junior-agent scenarios already expose this. 


 




E. Revision-loop attacks
Use the “customer changes everything” and “scope creep” patterns. The system must track deltas, not re-treat each message as a fresh brief. Otherwise it wastes agency time and hides time sink behavior. 


 




F. Multi-party attacks
Group trips with different budgets, payment terms, room preferences, or document status are where naive schemas break. This is where a simple traveler profile becomes insufficient and sub-group logic is forced. 


 




G. Audit-mode attacks
Messy PDF text, OTA copy, screenshots, missing cost buckets, implied inclusions, activity mismatch for elderly and toddlers. The audit engine should flag uncertainty and gaps, not hallucinate a clean plan out of dirty inputs. 


 




H. Commercial sabotage attacks
Feed options where traveler fit is good but agency margin is bad, or margin is good but fit is poor. The system should not optimize only one dimension. The product thesis is explicit that traveler fit, operational fit, and commercial fit must all be balanced. 


 




The test object you should create
Each real scenario should be turned into a gold test case with one strict template.


For each test, define:


Raw input
WhatsApp, voice transcript, note, URL extract, or uploaded itinerary text.


Prior context
customer history, agency policies, supplier memory, stage, and route state.


Expected canonical packet
facts, derived signals, hypotheses, unknowns, contradictions, provenance.


Expected decision state
PROCEED, ASK_FOLLOWUP, PRESENT_BRANCHES, or ESCALATE.


Expected questions or next action
top 1 to 3 follow-ups, or internal planning action.


Forbidden behaviors
what the model must not do.


This last field is important. For example:


Forbidden:
- Do not assume exact dates
- Do not propose Maldives as feasible at stated budget
- Do not ignore elderly mobility note
- Do not ask more than 3 questions
- Do not widen to open market before package/preferred path check
That gives you a real spec, not just examples. It also matches your modular prompting and decision-policy design. 


 


 




The whole process, end to end
This is the process I would use.


Phase 1: Build the gold corpus
Start with 10 high-pain scenarios, not all 30 at once.


Best first set:


P1-S1 11 PM WhatsApp panic


P1-S3 customer changes everything


P1-S4 visa/passport crisis


P2-S1 quote disaster review


P2-S3 margin erosion


P3-S1 first solo quote


P3-S2 visa mistake prevention


S1-S1 comparison shopper


S2-S1 preference conflict


S2-S3 budget reality check


Why these first: they cover intake, contradictions, blockers, quote quality, margin, customer experience, and group-fit logic. That is most of your product surface already. 


 


 


 




Phase 2: Lock Notebook 01
Run only extraction and normalization tests first. Do not let N02 or prompting muddy failures.


Success metrics here should be:


extraction accuracy


blocker precision


contradiction recall


provenance integrity


Those metrics are already implied in your first-mile success criteria. 




Phase 3: Lock Notebook 02
Now feed frozen canonical packets into the decision engine.


Metrics:


decision-state accuracy


follow-up question relevance


branch quality


escalation accuracy


This is where most brittle “agent behavior” really lives. 


 




Phase 4: Add prompt-bundle tests
Once N01 and N02 are stable, test that the final prompt bundle obeys the contract:


right stage


right missing fields


right number/order of questions


no forbidden assumptions


This is where prompt regression tests matter. 


 




Phase 5: Mutation tests
Now start breaking one variable at a time.
Change budget only.
Change traveler composition only.
Change dates only.
Change source authority only.
Change stage only.


This mirrors the autoresearch idea properly. Mutate one dimension, run the full harness, keep only measurable improvements. 




Phase 6: Shadow mode
Before any live rollout, run the system alongside a real agent on actual leads, but do not let it own the workflow. Compare its packet, decision, and questions against what the human did. Your real-world validation roadmap already points to shadow mode before pilot. 




Phase 7: Pilot and outcome checks
Only after shadow mode should you care about:


quote turnaround time


conversion rate


revision count


margin protection


fewer missed docs/visa errors


owner visibility


That is business validation, not model validation. Keep them separate. 


 




The key failure taxonomy I would adopt
Use one unified break taxonomy across the whole project:


False positive
System assumes or extracts something that is not true.


False negative
System misses something important that was there.


Contradiction blindness
System sees two conflicting signals and proceeds anyway.


Authority inversion
System trusts the wrong source.


Stage blindness
System gives the wrong action for the workflow stage.


Commercial blindness
Good traveler fit, bad agency viability.


Operational blindness
Looks good on paper, painful in execution.


Trust failure
System sounds certain when it should be uncertain.


This is cleaner than mixing QA categories randomly. Some of this is explicit in your scenario summary and some comes straight from the trust/failure docs. 


 


 




What I would not do
I would not start with:


fully open-ended “chat quality” evals


UI-only testing


synthetic happy-path fixtures only


too many specialist agents early


prompt tweaking before packet and decision contracts are stable


Your own docs already warn against premature agent sprawl and recommend specialization only after error clusters justify it. Correct. 


 




My blunt view
The real product risk is not “can the model write a nice itinerary.”
The real risk is “does the system know when it does not know, and does it stop bad agency action early enough.”


So the first serious test suite should mostly be stop-tests:


stop before quoting impossible Maldives


stop before visa disaster


stop before wrong-fit luxury quote


stop before group booking with missing docs


stop before wasting money on low-utility activities


If those gates work, you have something real. If they don’t, the rest is decoration. 


 


 


 




Next, I’d turn the first 10 scenarios into an exact gold-test template for Notebook 01 and Notebook 02, with expected packet fields, decision states, and forbidden behaviors.
